Change Title: Extending shelf life for RPMI1640, from 12 to 21 months
Change from:: Temporary change for extending the shelf life from 12 to 18 months for RPMI1640, cat: 041-94218 A lot: 3134836.
Change to: Extend expiry date of RPMI1640 cell culture medium from 2026-03 to 2026-12. Amount of remaining flasks of lot 3134836 with expiry date 2026-03: approx. 16-1800.
Justification for Change: Curida Diatec has currently over 1600 1L bottles of RPMI that expiry after 31.03.2026. The cell culture medium has a currently defined 12 month shelf life. The supplier confirms that the medium should retain its quality for at least additional six months, however some degradation in the concentration of some amino acids over time can be expected. The impact of extending the shelf life of the medium has previously been evaluated in collaboration with the product supplier. I addition, we have previously extended the shelf life of this medium and successfully completed production after expiry was extended. CMID1 was raised to extend shelf-life of RPMI tanks, and a risk assesment was performed. Some of the information is summarized below.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Two cell culture medium lots were tested in september 2021 to evaluate the impact of extending product shelf life on its quality (documents are available within CMID1). Lot 2167300 (sample 1") with expiration date 12-2020 was tested 9 months after passing its expiration date. Lot 2234714 ("sample 2") with expiration date 12-2021 was tested 4 months before passing its expiration date. The level of vitamins and amino acids was determined in both medium lots. From all tested molecules, only the level of L-Glutamine significantly decreased in the expired medium ("sample 1"), reaching approx. 40 % of the concentration after 21 months after production.No significant changes in vitamins or amino acids (except L-Glutamine) were observed in medium lot 2167200, indicationg that the medium will retain its quality for at least 9 months after passing its expiration date. Composition of RPMI 1L flasks and 100/200 L tanks is according to the same formula, and have the same amount of L-glutamine addition per liter. Supplier data indicating at least six additional months of acceptable quality aligns with our internal findings showing preserved medium performance for at least nine months beyond expiry.  L-Glutamine supports cells growth with high energy demands and synthesizes large amounts of proteins and nucleic acids. It it an alternative energy source for rapidly dividing cells and using glucose inefficiently. Low-level L-Glutamine amino acid can potentially cause decrees in the yield, but not the quality of the antibodies produced by the cells. The risk associated with using a cell culture medium with an extended shelf life is considered minor and is defined as a business risk only. The medium has been stored under the approved controlled temperature conditions, ensuring that any observed degradation is chemical in nature and not related to sterility or microbial growth. The medium will be supplemented with L-Glutamine before is use in production to reduce the risk of low yield. The Production team will carry the L-Glutamine supplementation according to standard procedur SOP2016, and to assist in calculation of the amount of L-glutamine to be added on the day the medium is prepared, a calculator based on the measurments done for the previous study, where a linea regression for L-glutamine degredation is used to determine how much glutamine to add, depending on the time passed from expiry date. This calculator is avaialble on Curida Diatecs Sharepoint site, and accessible within the cleanroom as well as the analytical lab, where medium is prepared.  Extending the shelf life does not introduce additional quality or safety risks, as only L‑Glutamine shows predictable degradation, which is fully mitigated through established supplementation procedures. The risk associated with an increasing amount of L-Glutamine degradation products (ammonium and pyroglutamate) is considered minor. The quality of the antibodies produced using this cell culture medium will not be affected. In-process controls will be performed, using CEDEX/ELISA to monitor the yield. In the worst scenario, if the yield will be significantly lower than expected, additional production units can be set up to increase production on the ompany cost. The cost savings from using this medium will offset the expense of potentially neding to start additional production units in certain cases. It is considered economically beneficial to extend shelf life for this medium and use it, as we have previously seen that it does not negatively affect production. "
Planned implementation data - comment: Change can be implemented when change actions 665-667 and 669 have been performed. As this is a temporary change, action 668 will be completed at the end of the change period, before closing the change.

Change Title: Implementation of HF primer bioreactor
Change from:: No small scale Hollow Fiber (HF) bioreactors are implemented at Curida Diatec. AcuSyst medium (Maximizer) and large scale (Xcellerator) platforms implemented.
Change to: Implement the HF Primer from Cell Culture Company, as a small scale HF bioreactor to make assessment of clone feasibility less labor and resource demanding.
Justification for Change: Curida Diatec currently lacks a small‑scale hollow‑fiber (HF) bioreactor suitable for clone feasibility studies. Existing workflows rely on either: - Membrane-based bioreactors (CL1000) for early clone assessment, or- Medium and large‑scale HF systems (Maximizer/Xcellerator) for production‑oriented evaluations.This results in high labor, resource consumption, and cost, especially when transferring clones from CL1000 to larger HF platforms. To address this gap, Curida Diatec will implement the HF Primer, a small‑scale HF bioreactor provided by Cell Culture Company (CCC). The HF Primer uses the same cartridge type as the Maximizer and Xcellerator platforms, allowing scalability from small to medium to large HF systems. doing this ensures:   - Establishing a small-scale HF model reduces material and labor use in clone selection for hollow fiber and minimizes uncertainty if the clone is suitable.- Ensures direct comparability between small, medium, and large HF performance due to identical cartridge architecture. - Enables more efficient clone screening, leading to potential cost savings and reduced timelines.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Product Quality: The HF Primer is same cartridge type as existing platforms, ensuring there is no impact to clones already using the HF. Improved data quality due to scalability (same cartridge type as large systems) for new clones, and no increased risk. Quality of each new clone will be assessed by standard quality control methods and trying clones on the HF is done in cooperation with customers.Production: Reduces time and cost of clone feasibility. Eliminates need for resource‑intensive large‑scale HF feasibility assessments.Equipment & Facility: HF Primer is compact and needs a specific rack, incubator with temperature and CO2 regulation, and a double headed pump to control in and out flow within the bioreactor. Curida Diatec has 6 CO2 incubators with temp and CO2 regulation, and plenty of space. several pumps are available, however a double headed pump head must be purchased (change action 661) and the flow of the pumps confirmed to be as expected (change action 660)Logistics: supplier is already qualified as a bioreactor manufacturer and criticality remains the same. Materials (HF primer and rack) need to be inserted into ERP system and ordered (change action 658) Safety: No new hazards, standard bioreactor handling practices apply.  Training: Appropriate training required prior to operational use and training module needs to be created. (change action 663 and 662)  Regulatory / Quality Systems: No impact on regulatory filings.Documentation: New SOPs, worksheets and training records required. (change action 659)     Risk AreaDescription of RiskSeverityLikelihoodTotal risk scoreMitigationSeverityLikelihoodRisk score after mitigationOperationalIncorrect setup or operation due to new equipment   2   2   4   Training + SOPs and worksheets (change action 663 and 659)   2   1   2   Data comparabilityResults not correlating as expected with larger HF systems   1   1   1   Use identical cartridge types, test run with a clone established on HF will be performed (change action 664)   1   1   1   Equipment performanceHF Primer not meeting expected performance   2   1   2   Test run will be performed (change action 664)   2   1   2   ContaminationBioreactor contamination impacting study outcome   2   1   2   Bioreactor will be run in the cleanroom and all operators working there are aseptically trained, no mitigating actions required.   2   1   2   Supply chainCartridges or consumables unavailable   1   2   2   Good relationship with supplier, established and known for good customer relations. Risk is accepted, no mitigation requried   1   2   2               Risk after mitigation: Acceptable

Change Title: Migration of Cellstock Inventory from SharePoint to Custom Application
Change from:: SharePoint List system with flat architecture. It suffers from a 5,000-item view threshold limit, leading to sorting failures and UI lag. It lacks a granular audit trail and is vulnerable to accidental data deletion requiring external IT (previously Braathe now smartIT) intervention.
Change to: Custom-built Application hosted on Firebase. Features a hierarchical database (Tank → Rack → Box → Position), built-in audit trails for every edit, automated 98-day backups, and seamless .csv exports for invoicing/reporting.
Justification for Change: Assessment justification: This involves a significant modification to a system managing critical materials (hybridoma cell stocks). It requires validation of data integrity and potential qualification of the software's performance. The migration to new data system CMID117 did not manage to bring all metadata surrounding the cellstock SharePoint list with it, requiring a new system to be built or put in place.  To resolve NC366 (operational inefficiencies in vial tracking) and NC191 (data integrity vulnerabilities). CAPA271 identified the Firebase app as the optimal balance of cost and customization.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Production: Improves speed and accuracy of retrieving/storing vials. Documentation Systems: Requires updates to the SOP governing inventory management.  Training: Personnel must be trained on the new UI.  Compliance: Ensures adherence to ISO 9001:2015 regarding data integrity and Control of Quality Records".    Identified Risks: Loss of Support: As an internal tool, there is no external help desk. Mitigation: Comprehensive technical documentation and source code must be stored in a shared Curida Diatec repository.  Data Migration Error: Risk of data corruption during the transfer of 10,000 items. Mitigation: Perform a "Test Migration" with a subset of data and a 100% verification check of the final production migration. This test will be performed with a double control (two operators checking simultaniously). "

Change Title: Implementation of EZ flask bioreactors
Change from:: Today, small‑scale antibody production at Curida Diatec relies exclusively on the CL1000 membrane-based bioreactor. This includes: A single bioreactor format  Frequent handling and cleanroom entries (membrane wetting, feeding, harvesting) High media use (10–20 L per run) Long production times (5–12 weeks) Documented challenges with CL1000 supply and membrane integrity
Change to: Introduce EZ‑flasks (1 L gas‑permeable bioreactor flasks) as an additional approved production format. This involves: Using EZ‑flasks in parallel with CL1000 for selected clones  Standard inoculation and culture steps performed in the cleanroom  A single-compartment culture format (1 L) with a gas‑exchange membrane in the base  Shorter production time (~30-50 days)  Lower media consumption (1-3 L)  One additional processing step before purification: harvest concentration via TFF
Justification for Change: Improved Robustness and Reduced DependencyCL1000 flasks have had repeated issues in recent years, including membrane breakage and delivery delays. With multiple non-conformances related to membrane failures since 2023, relying on a single type of bioreactor presents a clear operational risk. Introducing EZ‑flasks gives Curida Diatec an alternative technology and reduces vulnerability in the supply chain.   Cost and Operational EfficiencyProduction in EZ‑flasks is significantly more cost‑effective: ~65% lower total production cost compared to CL1000  Significantly fewer cleanroom entries and reduced use of disposable plastics  Lower operator time—approx. 4 hours per run compared to ~11–13 hours in CL1000     Comparable or Better Productivity for Several ClonesStudies RD262–RD264 showed that EO‑1 and My‑11 perform very well in EZ‑flasks, with yields matching or even approaching the CL1000 output on a cost‑adjusted basis. Although clone 581 produced less in the first EZ‑flask test, results indicate that this was due to the harvesting method rather than a fundamental issue with the flask.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Impacted AreasProduction: New inoculation and harvest workflow, TFF concentration added.  Downstream Processing: One extra step (TFF) before purification.  Procurement: New consumables (EZ‑flasks, TFF membranes).  Documentation: Updates to SOPs, batch records, material specifications.  Training: Operators must be trained in EZ‑flask handling and TFF concentration.     Risk AssessmentProduct differences: Moderate risk. Managed by running EZ‑flasks in parallel with CL1000 and performing QC comparability. Yield variability: Most relevant for clone 581. Mitigated by using the correct harvesting method and performing further optimization. New process step (TFF): Low–moderate risk. Mitigation includes operator training and standardizing the concentration step. Supply of EZ‑flasks: Low risk once a supplier agreement and safety stock are established. Documentation gaps during transition: Low risk, controlled through standard change management and review processes.   Overall residual risk after mitigation is acceptable and manageable, and significantly lower than continuing with a single vulnerable bioreactor format.

Change Title: CAPA Governance Alignment (ISO 9001:2015)
Change from:: 1. CAPA SOP 5039 v3.0 allows effectiveness evaluation to be optional, including for preventive actions, and permits QA to decide whether effectiveness evaluation is required for all CAPAs. 2. Work instruction does not clearly describe how to document effectiveness evaluation consistently for all CAPAs. 3. BizzMine CAPA workflow allows CAPAs to be closed without documented effectiveness evaluation.
Change to: 1. CAPA SOP 5039 (new version 4.0) requires effectiveness evaluation for all CAPAs using a risk-based and proportionate approach, and defines QA responsibility as setting the scope and method of effectiveness evaluation rather than deciding whether it is required. 2. Work instruction provides clear guidance and examples for documenting risk-based effectiveness evaluation, aligned with the revised CAPA SOP.  3. BizzMine CAPA workflow requires entry and QA approval of effectiveness evaluation prior to CAPA closure.
Justification for Change: This change is required to align CAPA documentation and system workflow with ISO 9001:2015 intent by removing optionality in effectiveness evaluation and ensuring consistent, risk-based application. The update improves governance clarity and audit robustness and was identified proactively during a planned SOP review, with no identified quality impact.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Impact Assessment: The change impacts CAPA governance documentation and system workflow only. It does not alter product design, manufacturing processes, testing activities, or service delivery. The update clarifies existing expectations by removing optionality in effectiveness evaluation and aligning SOP, work instruction, and BizzMine workflow requirements. No impact to product or service quality, customer satisfaction, or regulatory compliance is anticipated. Open CAPAs will continue to be managed under the procedure in effect at the time of approval, ensuring continuity and avoiding disruption.   Risk AssessmentThe overall risk associated with this change is assessed as low. Risk of not implementing the change:Potential for inconsistent application of effectiveness evaluation and reduced ability to demonstrate alignment with ISO 9001:2015 intent during audits.  Risk of implementing the change:Minor increase in documentation requirements for CAPA closure, mitigated through clear SOP wording, updated work instruction guidance, and system workflow controls.  Mitigating controls: Risk-based effectiveness evaluation (proportionate approach)   QA oversight and approval   User communication and training   Prospective application of new requirements    No new hazards, failure modes, or quality risks are introduced by this change.   Conclusion The change improves clarity, consistency, and audit robustness of the CAPA process with minimal operational impact. Existing quality system controls remain effective, and the residual risk after implementation is considered acceptable and low. Summary of planned actions to implement change: 1. Revise SOP 5039 to require effectiveness evaluation for all CAPAs using a risk-based approach and clarify QA responsibility as defining the scope and method of effectiveness evaluation. Update WIN 5039_1 to provide practical guidance on documenting effectiveness evaluation and acceptable evaluation methods, aligned with the revised SOP. 2. Modify and validate the BizzMine workflow to require documentation of effectiveness evaluation and prevent CAPA closure without QA-approved effectiveness assessment. 3. Deliver training to relevant stakeholders on the updated procedure and workflow.
Planned implementation data - comment: The 31 March 2026 implementation date is appropriate given the low-risk, documentation-focused nature of the change and the need to update the SOP, work instruction, and BizzMine workflow in a controlled manner, including verification and user communication, while maintaining continuity for open CAPAs.

Change Title: Implementation of Data Systems module in BizzMine
Change from:: Current management of Data Systems (e.g. BizzMine, Sirius, Nova) lacks a centralized, validated register within the eQMS. Updates to these systems are currently not tracked in a dedicated lifecycle module, making it difficult to distinguish between minor patches and major updates requiring full change control.
Change to: Implementation of a new Data Systems" list module within BizzMine. This module will serve as a qualified inventory for all data systems and a log for tracking software updates. It will allow for a risk-based approach where minor updates are justified and logged directly in the module, while major updates are linked to full Change Control and Validation procedures"
Justification for Change: Rationale: The introduction of the Data Systems" module aligns with the Risk-Based Approach described in the Quality Manual. It provides a structured mechanism to:Enhance Traceability: Create a single source of truth for the status and version history of critical systems (BizzMine, Sirius, Nova).Improve Efficiency: Streamline the handling of minor software patches. By documenting a risk assessment within the module to confirm an update is "minor" (low impact), the organization can implement patches without initiating a full Change Control process for every minor iteration, while maintaining full audit trails.Ensure Compliance: Major updates affecting compliance or validation status will remain linked to the formal Change Management process (SOP 5038), ensuring ISO 9001 compliance regarding infrastructure and documentation"
Impacted areas of the organization and r: This change is categorized as Major since it requires additional validation activities for implementing a new module within the eQMS (BizzMine) to ensure data integrity and correct workflow functionality.    BizzMine (eQMS) Impact: Creation of a new Data Systems" collection/form and configuration of workflows. This will also include a form collection for updates that will be placed within the Data Systems collection. CA635 Mitigation: Validate the new module (Installation/Operational Qualification) to ensure fields, permissions, and audit trails function as intended. CA636   Validation & Qualification Impact: The module itself acts as a tool for validation maintenance. The setup of the module requires validation.  Mitigation: Execute a Validation for the new module before release. CA636   Documentation Systems Impact: Need for instructions on how to use the new module. Mitigation: Create a new Working Instruction (WIN) or SOP for the "Data Systems" module workflow. CA637   Training & Personnel Impact: Relevant personnel (System Owners, QA) need to know how to register updates. CA638 Mitigation: Conduct training on the new module and the new process for logging minor vs. major updates."
Planned implementation data - comment: The implementation includes having the modules tested and validated and SOPs written and completed.

Change Title: Consolidation of Batch Numbering in production series
Change from:: Current practice assigns a unique Batch Number to every individual CL1000 single-use flask, even when processed simultaneously in a series (which consist of up to 6 flasks) on a single worksheet.
Change to: Implementation of a Series Batch Number" system where one single Batch Number is assigned to a production series (up to 6 flasks processed together). Individual traceability within the batch will be maintained via sub-identifiers (e.g., Batch BXXXX-Flask 1, Batch BXXXX-Flask 2) recorded in the batch documentation."
Justification for Change: This change is administrative regarding registration and identification. It does not alter the physical manufacturing process, raw materials, or equipment parameters, nor does it impact product quality or safety.   Efficiency & Accuracy: The current system creates redundant data entry in BizzMine which is the system that is going to be used for batch registration (CMID112). Consolidating to one batch number per series simplifies registration and reduces the risk of human error during manual entry.System Interconnectivity: A single batch number allows for better linking of data (IPC results, materials, time logs) within the BizzMine eQMS, as the data applies to the whole series.Process Alignment: The current IPC analysis is already performed collectively (one sample representing the series). Aligning the batch number with the sampling plan reflects the scientific reality of the process.Traceability: Traceability is maintained. In the event of leakage or infection in a single flask, the sub-identifier (Flask ID) allows for the segregation and rejection of that specific portion of the batch without compromising the entire series.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Production: Positive Impact. Reduces administrative burden on operators.Risk: Operators might forget to label physical flasks with the sub-identifier (Flask 1-6).Documentation Systems:The batch registration system currently in excel needs to include a column indicating number of flasks.Quality Control:Impact: IPC results will now formally link to one batch number rather than implying it applies to six separate numbers. This will benefit batch review.BizzMine (eQMS): The form for registration of batches must be able to use this new string format. The batch registration system is migrating from excel to BizzMine (CMID112), this new module must also contain option of number of flasks.Customers: NA   HSE: NA   Equipment, Facility: NA

Change Title: Implement Endosafe nextgen-PTS
Change from:: Curida Diatec is currently not in possesion of a Endotoxin tester and hence needs external endotoxin testing of samples.
Change to: Implement Endosafe PTS150
Justification for Change: By implementing an in-house endotoxin testing capability using an Endosafe system from Charles River may provide quality and operational benefits compared to outsourcing testing to an external laboratory. In-house testing will potentially reduce turnaround time, enabling same-day results and supporting timely batch disposition, deviation investigations, and/or process troubleshooting. This might lead to improved workflow efficiency and reduces the risk of delays caused by sample shipment and external laboratory queues. Performing testing internally provides greater control over testing conditions, scheduling, and data handling, reducing dependency on third parties and minimizing the risk of errors, miscommunication, or sample integrity issues associated with transport and external handling. From a quality perspective, in-house testing supports improved sample integrity by eliminating shipping-related risks such as temperature excursions or delays. It also enhances data traceability and confidentiality, as all testing activities and results remain within the company’s quality system.   Overall, establishing in-house endotoxin testing with an Endosafe system strengthens quality control, improves responsiveness, and supports compliance with GMP expectations for timely and controlled release testing.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Implementing the Endosafe® endotoxin testing system from Charles River will impact areas of the diagnostic site.The main impacts are assessed and summarised below: 1. FacilityFacility requirements for the Endosafe® system are limited to standard laboratory conditions, including bench space, power supply, and controlled environment consistent with existing QC laboratory infrastructure. No facility modifications are anticipated. IQ,OQ and PQ shall be tranfered with cover sheet. See change action 642. Fixed 25 µl Thermo scientific pipette will also be implemented for use on Endosafe® system. This will be implemented according to SOP3002 and calibrated. See change action 643. 2. HSENo significant health, safety, or environmental hazards have been identified. The Endosafe® system does not involve hazardous chemicals, high pressures, or elevated temperatures. Handling and disposal of cartridges and reagents will follow existing laboratory HSE procedures. Overall risk is assessed as low. 3. SuppliersCharles River Laboratories or similar consumable provider will supply cartridges and related consumables, as well as service and technical support. Charles River will need to be qualified and approved as supplier. See relevant change action 639.  4. Quality Management System (QMS)Implementation of the instrument will be managed in accordance with the equipment management procedure (ref. SOP 3002). An instrument implementation form in Bizzmine must be completed and approved for the Endosafe system and associated components. Relevant SOPs and controlled documents related to endotoxin testing, sample handling will be reviewed and updated as required to reflect in-house testing. See relevant change action 641. 5. PersonnelProduction personnel will receive training in operation, testing procedures, data handling, and/or basic troubleshooting of the Endosafe system. Training will be documented and managed in accordance with the local training management procedure (SOP 5047). Only trained and qualified personnel will be authorized to perform testing. See relevant change action 640. 6. LogisticsRequired consumables (e.g. endotoxin test cartridges and controls) are clearly defined by the supplier. See related action above. 7. Change Title: Implementation of ​Milli-Q® IX 7005 purification system + 100 l tank ref WO-03099346
Change from:: The diagnostic facility is currently using a Millipore Elix 3 water purification system to supply Type II purified water for general laboratory operations, including preparation of buffers, incubator water bath and cleaning of glassware
Change to: implement a new water purification system from Merck, the Milli-Q IX 7005 water purification system.
Justification for Change: The current system, Millipore Elix 3, has been in use for 17 years and is approaching the end of its operational life. As a consequence, the system is prone to breakdown.  Additionally, the current system's production capacity of 3 L/hour no longer meets the growing water demand(growth) of the laboratory, leading to workflow inefficiencies and longer wait times for purified water. To address these limitations, we are  upgrading to a water purifying system, which offers a production rate of ≥5L/hour of high-quality Type II water. This increase in capacity will support current and future needs of the lab and ensure uninterrupted water availability for daily operations. In addition with the system upgrade, we also will continue using a 100-liter storage tank to buffer the purified water output and provide a reserve supply during peak-usage times. The combination of a higher-capacity purification unit and adequate storage will significantly improve the lab's efficiency, reduce equipment strain, and support compliance with quality production standards. This investment ensures long-term operational reliability, improved productivity, and future scalability, aligning with both laboratory performance goals and sustainability objectives by leveraging modern, energy-efficient water purification technologies.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Implementing the Milli-Q IX 7005 water purification system will impact areas of the diagnostic site.  The main impact is assessed and summarised below: 1. FacilityFacility requirements are similar to the current water purification setup and are expected to be fully compatible with existing infrastructure. This will be verified and documented during the Installation Qualification (IQ). See Change action 612.2. HSENo significant hazards related to health, safety, or environment are identified. The system does not involve high operating pressures, hazardous chemicals, or other elevated risks beyond standard laboratory equipment. Any minor handling of consumables will follow established HSE procedures.3. SuppliersMerck will supply consumables and provide service and support for the system. Merck is an approved supplier and their qualification status is confirmed.4. Quality Management System (QMS)Implementation of the instrument will comply with the equipment management procedure (ref. SOP 3002). An instrument implementation form in Bizzmine must be completed and approved for the Milli-Q IX 7005 and associated components. See change action 613.SOP 4019 and DOC 4019.1 require revision to reflect the introduction of the new water purification system. See change action 614.5. PersonnelAdequate training must be provided to affected production operators. A training module already exists for water purification systems and will be updated as needed, in accordance with the local training management procedure (SOP 5047).6. LogisticsRequired consumables have been clearly defined and are included in the Merck offer. Since Merck is already an approved supplier, no additional supplier qualification activities are required.7. Qualification / ValidationIt has been assessed that a Performance Qualification (PQ) is not required for this system. The Milli-Q IX 7005 is a standardized, commercially available system that reliably produces water within defined purity specifications when installed and operated according to manufacturer guidelines. System suitability will be demonstrated through IQ and OQ, which will verify correct installation, configuration, and operational performance against approved acceptance criteria.Additionally, water quality will be subject to routine monitoring and control during ongoing use, ensuring continuous confirmation of system performance. As there are no unique product-specific performance requirements beyond the defined water quality specifications, a PQ would not contribute additional assurance of fitness for intended use. Therefore, IQ and OQ are considered adequate and compliant with SOP 5018.IQ and OQ activities will be performed by qualified personnel from Curida Diatec. See change action 612.8. ProductionThe system is functionally comparable to the current Merck water purification unit used in diagnostic manufacturing. Only minor training in the updated user interface will be necessary for the operators. See change action 615.

Change Title: Transition of Batch Release Checklists to BizzMine Electronic Form
Change from:: The current batch release process relies on printing and manually filling one of four separate Microsoft Word documents: DOC5051.1, DOC5051.2, DOC5051.3, and DOC5051.4. Each document is a checklist tailored to a specific production type. These completed paper forms serve as quality records for batch release decisions.
Change to: The four separate checklists will be consolidated and replaced by a single, dynamic electronic form within the BizzMine eQMS. This form will be filled out and approved electronically for each batch. The completed electronic form will become the official quality record for the batch release, ensuring data integrity and control within a validated system.
Justification for Change: This change is proposed to improve the effectiveness of our Quality System and to create a more smooth batch release process for QA personnel.  Benefits to this change are: Efficiency: Eliminates the need for printing, manual data entry, scanning, and physical storage, thereby streamlining the workflow for the Quality Assurance team. Traceability: Centralizes all batch release records in the BizzMine system, making them instantly searchable and retrievable. This aligns with our QMS requirement for legible, identifiable, and retrievable quality records. Data Analysis and KPIs: Enables the collection of structured data directly from the release process. This data can be used to monitor Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), such as release cycle time, type of errors in batch documentation, and to analyze trends for continual improvement, which is a core part of Diatec's Quality Policy. Process Control: Reduces the risk of using obsolete forms and ensures all required fields are completed before a release decision can be finalized, improving Good Documentation Practice.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Production & Quality Control: Low risk. Staff who provide input for the release will need to be aware of the new process, but their core activities are unchanged.   Quality Assurance: High impact. This directly changes a core process for the QA team. Risk: The electronic form may not capture all necessary checks from the four original documents.    Mitigation: The BizzMine form must be developed and validated against all requirements of DOC5051.1, .2, .3, and .4.      Validation and Qualification: High impact. The new BizzMine form must be formally validated to ensure it is fit for purpose. Risk: The validation is insufficient, leading to a non-compliant electronic record system.    Mitigation: A validation plan and report must be created according to SOP5018.      Training and Personnel: Medium impact. All personnel involved in the batch release process will require training on the new BizzMine form and workflow. Risk: Personnel may not use the new system correctly, leading to errors.    Mitigation: Formal training must be conducted and documented before the change becomes effective.      Documentation Systems: The existing documents (DOC5051.1-4) must be made obsolete. The main SOP for product release (SOP5011)  will need to be updated to reflect the new electronic process.
Planned implementation data - comment: CA606 will be implemented after the change has been implemented.

Change Title: Implementation of 100 kDa Amicon Spin Columns for IgM Antibody Upconcentration and polishing
Change from:: 30 KDa and 50 kDa MWCO Amicon Spin column used in SOP0070
Change to: SOP0070 expanded to include 100 kDa Amicon spincolumns
Justification for Change: Current antibody upconcentration protocols utilize Amicon spin columns with 30 kDa and 50 kDa molecular weight cut-offs (MWCO), optimized for IgG antibodies (~150 kDa). However, these filters are not suitable for IgM antibodies (~900 kDa), especially when contaminants such as bovine serum albumin (BSA) are present. Therefore we Amicon Ultra spin columns with 100 kDa MWCO will be implemented into the antibody purification workflow for IgM samples. This change will be documented in SOP0070. The method described in SOP0070m using Amicon spin column is a general method used for concentration of antibodies, but as according to materials information and previous experience with products in-house, can also be used to polish final product by removing impurities as the membrane has a specific MW cut off (impurities below column MWCO size are removed), by implementing 100 kDa it will be possible to use 30, 50 and 100 kDa spin columns for this purpose. This change is driven by a customer-specific IgM product that has been confirmed to contain BSA contamination (CC1 and CC5). The BSA is not effectively removed using 30 or 50 kDa spin filters as it is over 50 kDa in size. Since IgM is a large molecule (~900 kDa), it is not affinity purified but processed via CaptoCore 700 SEC, which has limited separation efficiency at this size range. Implementing 100 kDa filters will allow for improved removal of BSA while retaining IgM, enhancing product purity and consistency. This implementation is requested by customer.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Amicon spin columns have been used by Curida Diatec for years, and indirectly also been used for polishing. There is a risk that 100 kDa will be used for IgGs and due to size, excess amount of product can be lost. SOP0070 wil lbe updated with instructions for 100 kDa spin columns to be used only for IgMs (change action 600) Impact assessment: Process Impact: Minimal changes as existing equipment is compatible and process is similar as before. SOP0070 will be updated with instructions on how to handle 100 kDa columns according to manual (change action 600) Product Impact: Expected improvement in IgM purity. Quality Impact: No negative impact anticipated, routine SDS-Page QC testing will confirm integrity of product. Logistics: ordering of the 100K MWCO Amicon spin column. The columns have already been ordered and are waiting for implementation of process. this material does not require Specification of incoming goods. Documentation and eQMS: SOP0070 needs to be updated to reflect use of 100 kDa spin columns (change action 600) Training: Operators to perform this method must be trained prior to executing the method (change action 601) HSE: no impact on HSE, as the material is same as previously used, no specifc HSE impact is identified
Planned implementation data - comment: Change is implemented when all actions have been completed.

Change Title: Inclusion of section for referencing previously released batch documentation in Control Plans
Change from:: Control plans not including a section to reference previously released documentation. Currently, when a production batch uses a component (e.g., a buffer or intermediate solution) that was produced and released under a separate, previous batch record, the technical verification team must print or copy the relevant documentation from the previous batch. These copied documents are physically added to the new batch record, then copy-stamped, signed, and re-reviewed by the Quality Assurance (QA) team during the final release process. This is a manual, time-consuming, and resource-intensive activity.
Change to: Revise the standard template for production Control Plans to include a new, dedicated section for referencing QA-Released documentation. Instead of physically copying documents, personnel will record the unique identification (e.g., run number, batch number) of the previously QA-released material/documentation in this section. During batch review, the technical verification and QA teams will verify the status of the referenced batch in the system, confirming it was released, rather than re-reviewing a physical copy.   This change is proposed to improve the efficiency and robustness of our documentation practices, in line with our goal of continual improvement. Benefits: Increased Efficiency: Significantly reduces the administrative workload for both the technical verification and QA teams, allowing them to focus on more critical review tasks. Reduced Waste: Eliminates the need for excessive printing and paper consumption. Improved Data Integrity: Reduces the risk of errors associated with photocopying or transcribing information. Traceability is maintained through a direct reference to the unique record of the original batch. Risk: A minor risk exists of personnel entering an incorrect batch number in the reference section. This is comparable to other data entry risks and will be mitigated through the standard verification and review process.
Justification for Change: The technical verification team currently needs to spend an exorbitant amount of time printing out or copying pages from previously released documents then copy-stamping them with individual date/sign for every page. Following this the QA team needs to verify that every page has been dated and signed properly.  Having a dedicated field in the control plan for the documentation of the released batch will maintain traceability as all batch documentation is digitally saved in the eQMS system without having to create excessive physical documentation.    The change is a modification to a documentation process and does not alter the manufacturing method, raw materials, equipment, or final product specifications. It is unlikely to have a significant impact on product or process quality and does not require new validation or qualification activities.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Production & Quality Control: The change will positively impact the efficiency of the documentation and release process. There is no impact on the physical manufacturing process or the quality control testing of the product.Documentation Systems: Impacted. The templates for production Control Plans must be updated. Training and Personnel: Impacted. Personnel in Production, Technical Verification, and Quality Assurance who are involved in completing or reviewing batch documentation will require training on the new procedure.Customers: No Impact. This is an internal process optimization that does not affect product specifications, quality, or services provided to the customer. Based on SOP5038, customer notification is not required as this change does not alter the product or supply chain.Compliance: No Impact. Traceability, a key requirement of ISO9001 and other relevant standards (i.e. GMP), is fully maintained. The system of referencing a unique, unalterable QA-released record is a robust method of demonstrating conformity.Facility, Equipment, Validation: No impact HSE: Some environmental benefit with less use of paper and energy.
Planned implementation data - comment: NA

Change Title: Implementation of Corning CL1000 Disposable Bioreactor Flasks as Alternative to Wheaton CL1000
Change from:: Use of Wheaton CELLine™ CL1000 flasks as the sole disposable bioreactor for antibody production. These flasks, provided by Fisher Scientific  and VWR, have historically shown issues with membrane integrity and contamination (ref. NC327, 310, 309, 298, 250, 235, 202). Supply has also been disrupted due to manufacturing issues at DWK Life Sciences.
Change to: Inclusion of Corning CL1000 disposable bioreactor flasks as an alternative option for production. Corning flasks are functionally equivalent to Wheaton flasks, with the same dual-membrane design and performance specifications. Materials differ slightly (e.g., source of resin and plastic), but flask format, usage, and process handling remain unchanged.
Justification for Change: Quality concerns with Wheaton CL1000 flasks (leaks, infections) and supply chain vulnerability due to production halts by current manufacturer.   Feasibility study (Attachment 1) results confirm that Corning flasks:  - Produce comparable yields across 3 batches per cell line.   - Yield consistent antibody concentration within expected variation.   - Meet all QC specifications, including SDS-PAGE, ELISA specificity, and endotoxin testing.    Operational continuity and flexibility are improved by qualifying an alternative supplier.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Impacted AreasArea  DescriptionProduction     Change in supplier of bioreactor flask, otherwise no process modification. Flask lot.no is noted  in batch documentation    Quality Control     IPC and QC testing continue as per current SOPs.No impact on current processes.   Procurement     Corning is purchased through approved supplier VWR/Avantor. Specfication of incoming goods must be made for Corning flask. (change action 576)   QMS     Change control record required, update worksheet to include cat.no of bioreactor for CL1000 production to ensure traceability, no other change to existing SOPs or product specifications.(change action 578)   Customers     No impact on customers as bioreactors are practically the same, and will be used in the same way. It should be evaluated if customers should be notified (change action 577)     Risk Assessment RiskMitigationSlight yield variation in product yield          Within acceptable range, all productions are monitoried for yield, therefore detectability is high and production timeline can be extended to compensate (evaluation and adjustment of production is standard process)   Handling differences   None observed, same format and process   Supply chain inconsistency   Mitigated by dual-sourcing approach   Quality deviation   All QC results from feasibility study passed and flasks perform similar as to DWK flasks.
Planned implementation data - comment: All actions must be completed prior to implementation of flasks.

Change Title: Implementation of Mobius CellReady 3L bioreactor and Applikon my-control bioreactor controller in ISO production
Change from:: At the ISO production site at Fornebu monoclonal antibodies are produced in: CL1000, AcuSyst Maximiser and AcuSyst-Xcellerator.
Change to: Implementation of Mobius CellReady single use 3L bioreactor and my-control bioreactor controller for production of  monoclonal antibodies at the Diatec ISO site at Fornebu.
Justification for Change: Stirred tank cultivation is a novel technology platform for  production of mABs at Curida and opens up for new business opportunities.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Implementing the Mobious CellReady 3L bioreactor and Applikon my-control controller will impact several areas of the ISO site. The main impact is assessed and summarised below: 1. Facility: facility requirements will be defined and addressed during installation qualification. There will be a need to move CO2 and O2 tanks into room 106 and obtain a source of pressurized air (compressor or supply line).   2. HSE – hazard to health, safety and environment should be assessed, and appropriate steps should be taken.  The main hazard to human health and safety are considered:   - Risk related to handling biological material As most production at the ISO site is related to biological material, all operators are trained in safely handling biological materials, the topic is covered by HSE training performed at the ISO site. Therefore there are  no additional risk to personnel related to handling biological material.   - Risks related to handling CO2 gas All operators are trained in handling CO2 gas, the topic is covered by HSE training all operators must complete. Therefore no additional risk to personnel related to this change. HMS aspects will be partially covered by operator training and instrument SOP   b) The main hazard to the environment: - Risk associated to waste management, including used biological medium and bioreactors consumables All consumable parts used in production will be utilized according to the local procedure for waste management (SOP4006).   - The risk associated with handling biological material and the risk of biological contamination Mobius CellReady 3L will not increase the risk of biological contaminants in the production facility. The Mobius CellReady 3L bioreactor is a closed system, which highly limits the production facility's risk of biological contamination. After cell inoculation, there is no higher risk of contamination from an Mobius CellReady 3L than already used Xcellerator or Maximiser instruments. Environment monitoring is performed in the production facility according to a local procedure (SOP 1076). The result of the monitoring conducted during test production will be inspected if instrument implementation did cause an increased risk of biological contamination in the production area.  Installation of pressurized air requires a compressor. This can in theory lead to more movement in the air and a higher particle count.  The topic will be addressed in change action 587.   3. Suppliers BioNordika/Getinge will provide instrument service and calibration for the Applikon my-control controller. Merck will provide consumables and the 3L CellReady single-use bioreactor. The qualification status of the suppliers are approved.   4. QMS  Implementation of the instrument should comply with the local procedure for Equipment (ref SOP 5018). The instrument implementation form should be prepared and approved for Mobius CellReady 3L , Applikon my-control  and supporting instruments that will be used in the production process.  The Instrument implementation form (DOC 5043.2) covers the following aspects of the QMS: instrument labeling, maintenance and calibration, documentation, and training.   5. Personnel – Sufficient training should be provided for production operators. The training module should be established according to the local procedure for training handling (SOP 5047). The training records should be prepared and approved for all trained operators. This is addressed in CA 586   6. Logistic – All consumables must be defined and categorized according to the local procedure for incoming material control (SOP 5010). The specification should include a quality control approach and release criteria. In-house production of reagents, media and additions required for the process is described in DOC0092.1   7. Qualification/Validation – The instrument will be used in the manufacturing process of monoclonals antibodies. Therefore, a complete instrument qualification should be performed according to the local procedure for Qualification and Validation activities (SOP 5018).   The qualification should be divided into two steps:  a) Installation & operational qualification – performed by personell from Curida Diatec The topic will be addressed in change action 584   c) performance qualification – performed as a test production using CHO clone 226 . The topic will be addressed in change action 585   8. Production - Instrument: The instrument is different from the existing bioreactors currently used in ISO production. The operators will need to receive training appropriate training and support. - Expansion of cells: The cells are expanded in shake flasks in a shaking incubator. The process is straightforward and passages are done by increasing flask and medium volumes until the desired cell concentration is reached. - IT security: as the Applikon my-control controller is connected to LAN for remote monitoring, unauthorized access to production data and tampering is possible. However, all access outside of Diatec is protected by a firewall administered by Diatecs IT provider, Braathe. Only designated computers (that contain a linked Teamviewer account) can access the PC and log in from outside the facility.
Planned implementation data - comment: Implement 3L bioractor in ISO production

Change from:: SOP3000 states that: the diagnostic production cleanroom suite and 3rd floor cleanroom requires annual requalification. The cleanrooms are not formally classified, however, the initial qualification of the cleanroom is according to ISO14644-3 class 7. Hence the 3rd floor cleanroom requires annual cleanroom requalification involving:  -Airflow Rate test (m3 /h)  -Exchange Rate (number of air changes per hour) -HEPA Filter Integrity Test  performed by approved supplier.
Change to: Temporary change: Postponement of annual requalification of the 3rd floor non-operational cleanroom.  The cleanroom is currently not in use for production activities. As such, postponing the scheduled annual requalification is proposed until the cleanroom is reinstated for manufacturing use.   10.APR.2026 JEL: Extend deadline for actions 562 and 563 as the cleanroom is not planned for implementation in manufactoring use before at least end of Q1 2027.
Justification for Change: Reduced cost and resource allocation during downtime, ensures requalification is meaningful and cost-effective when production resumes.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: RiskLikelihood       ImpactMitigationDegradation of cleanroom conditions due to lack of monitoring   Low Moderate     Continued routine cleaning per SOP1102, weekly EM (if used) according to SOP1076, monthly ventilation checks Contamination risk when personnel enter the cleanroom Low Low Full gowning protocols maintained according to SOP1095 System failure in HVAC or environmental control Low High Monthly checks on ventilation systems remain in place Use of non-requalified cleanroom in future manufacturing Medium High Cleanroom will be fully requalified, according to SOP3000 by approved supplier before resuming manufacturing Given the cleanroom is not in use for production and current maintenace practices (cleaning, gowning, EM, HVAC monitoring) are maintained, the risks associated with postponing the requalification are minimal. The major risk—resuming manufacturing in an unqualified environment—is controlled through a strict requirement for requalification before any production restart.   Compliance Impact:Postponement of requalification deviates from routine annual scheduling but does not pose compliance risk since the cleanroom is not in operational use and controls are in place. The change is temporary and reversible. Product Quality Impact:No impact. No products are being manufactured in the cleanroom at this time. Operational Impact:Reduced cost and resource allocation during downtime, ensures requalification is meaningful and cost-effective when production resumes.   Environmental Monitoring and Maintenance Continuity: - Gowning: Maintained according to current SOPs  - Cleaning: Maintained per schedule  - Ventilation: Monitored monthly  - EM: Conducted weekly if personnel are present    Given that the cleanroom is not in production use and all critical maintenance and control measures are upheld, the postponement of the annual requalification poses negligible risk to product quality or compliance.  It is proposed that the annual requalification be deferred and rescheduled prior to any future production activity, at which time the cleanroom will undergo requalification according to SOP3001 by an approved supplier.

Change Title: Change in disinfection procedure for CL1000 flasks
Change from:: The routine for disinfection CL1000 flasks described in SOP0042 v. 3.0 states to wipe each CL1000 flask with klerwipe peroxide on all sides except under the flask, then spray and wipe with sterile filtered ethanol before transferring the flask into the LAF bench.
Change to: All material transferred into the LAF bench are disinfected by spraying and wiping with sterile filtered ethanol. We propose that this standard procedure is also applied to CL1000 flasks. In addition, all CL1000 flasks will be wiped with peroxide once during monthly cleaning of incubators.
Justification for Change: This change is proposed to formally update the disinfection procedure for CL1000 flasks before transferring them into the LAF bench. QRM2020-124 was initiated 12.03.2020 due to the COVID-19 outburst in Europe and the risk of problem with delivery of disinfectants. For a period the disinfection routine for CL1000 flasks was reduced from wiping with peroxide then spaying and wiping with ethanol before transferring the flasks into the LAF bench, to disinfected with ethanol only before they were transferred into the LAF bench, and wipe with peroxide one a month during monthly cleaning of the incubators. During the time this practice was followed, no increase in the number of contamination/infections or change in the results for environmental monitoring was observed.    CAPA109 was created in relation to NC106 to update SOP0042 to continue the reduced disinfection of CL1000 flasks. NC106 was raised when it was discovered that disinfection was still being performed using only ethanol, even though the QRM2020-124 was intended to be temporary and SOP0042 still stated that the disinfection should be done with both peroxide and ethanol. However, NC106 was closed and CAPA109 was not approved for initiation after the investigation for NC106 showed that QRM2020-124 was still valid. QRM202-124 was temporary and should have been closed when the delivery situation of disinfectants was back to normal.    Since the practice has proven to be both effective and reliable, and also time-saving, cost-saving and improves health and safety conditions for the operators, we propose updating the procedure to allow disinfection with ethanol only before transferring CL1000 flasks into the LAF bench.  Formalizing this change ensures alignment across procedures and simplifies routine operations.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Impact assessment: 1. Impact on facility, utility, equipment: No impact on facility, utility or equipment.    2. Production No direct impact on manufacturing process.    3. Quality control: No changes to quality control.    4. Logistics No change for logistics other than the adjustment of the need for peroxide and ethanol.   5. Validation and qualification: No impact on any validation status  Simplifying the disinfection routine will also be more cost and time effective.    6. HSE:  No impact on HSE. Peroxide can irritate skin, eye and respiratory system and repeated exposure may have negative long term health affect, so reduced peroxide exposure is an advantage in A HSE perspective.    7. Training and personal.  Production personnel have to be aware of this change to ensure correct disinfection.  Any potential use of peroxide in addition to ethanol for disinfection of CL1000 flasks before transporting into the LAF bench poses no risk to the product.    8. Documentation Systems SOP0042 and SOP4008 needs to be updated.   9. Customer. No impact for customer. Change notification is not needed.    Risk: The disinfection effect might be decreased, this might increase the risk of infections.  We have previous experience showing that the contamination/infection rate does not change even when disinfection is done only using ethanol before the CL1000 flasks are transferred into the LAF bench.  Ethanol is an effective and widely used disinfectant in cleanroom, and all other materials transferred into our LAF benches are disinfected using only ethanol.  The product quality will remain the same, and in the event of an infection, it will be detected before the product is finalized, just as it would be if disinfection were done using both peroxide and ethanol.
Planned implementation data - comment: NA

Change Title: Update of Out of Spec procedure SOP5037
Change from:: The out-of-specification procedure SOP5037 does not apply for production activities conducted at the Diagnostic site (SOP5037), version 2.0 valid on 15.02.2022: This SOP applies to the investigation of OOS results for all GMP testing.
Change to: Updating out-of-specification procedure SOP5037 to apply to non-GMP activities performed at the Diagnostic site, which reflect the current process for OOS handling.
Justification for Change: The current procedure instructs on handling Out-of-Specification (OOS) results based on the Guidance for Industry - Investigating Out-of-Specification (OOS) Test Results for Pharmaceutical Production (October 2006) from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, which applies only to GMP activities. At the Diagnostics site, we have been consistently registering and documenting all potential OOS events within the nonconformances collection. This document update will align the procedure with our existing practices and ensure that documentation is consistent across all cases. Moreover, the FDA-based procedure is overly complex and challenging for our environment, making it difficult for operators to fully comprehend and effectively implement. By simplifying the process, we enable a more streamlined approach that is easier for operators to follow, ensures consistent reporting across all nonconformances, and minimizes the risk of oversight during batch revisions. The transition to using nonconformances for OOS handling offers multiple advantages, including enhanced ease of use, improved operational efficiency, and more consistent documentation that better reflects our practices.

Change to: Using a newly established and validated method with MACSQuant 10 (Miltenyi Biotec) made available by Sharelab, for analysis of  product 3226 and 3022.
Justification for Change: The current FACSCalibur flow cytometer used for quality control of leukocyte markers has been discontinued and is no longer supported by the manufacturer. Replacing the FACSCalibur is not only a technical upgrade, but a necessary step for business continuity, regulatory compliance, and future-proofing our flow cytometry capabilities. The switch to a modern cytometer will ensure assay reliability, better operational efficiency, and alignment with current industry standards.     A feasibility run (attachment 1)  was performed on MACSQuant 10 at Sharelab facility with same sample prep and specification as the products 3226 and 3022 have on FACS Calibur. According to the data, analysis  passed and instrument operated well.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Facility: As the instrument will be housed and operated off-site it will not affect the physical facilities at Curida Diatec. However, because the external laboratory is not under Curida’s control, it is critical to ensure that the facility meets all necessary quality and safety standards. The owner of the instrument must be qualified as an approved supplier according to Curida Diatec's procedures (CA548). Additionally, the external laboratory must be assessed for basic safety and hygiene practices. HSE alignment, access control, and emergency preparedness at the external site should be verified and recorded (CA551).    Equipment:     This change introduces a new flow cytometer to replace the current FACS Calibur, which presents a high risk due to differences in configuration, ownership, and qualification status. To mitigate this, a formal feasibility study was conducted (reports attachment 1 and 2) and confirmed to be able to quality control the product to at least the same standard as FACS CAlibur.. An agreement with the instrument owner will be established, clearly detailing maintenance responsibilities, calibration schedules, usage logs, scheduling and access arrangements, and cost of use (CA552). An SOP for the instrument will be created, covering cleaning, routine checks, troubleshooting, and shutdown/startup procedures (CA553).     Production: According to results from the feasibility study, the new instrument performs equally well or better than the FACS Calibur, and fits within existing QC acceptance criteria for product 3226. Therefore, the change is not expected to impact release criteria or batch disposition. Nonetheless, potential delays in QC due to scheduling conflicts, instrument downtime, or technical issues could impact production timelines. This risk is considered low as production schedules are planned several weeks in advance, allowing for flexibility.     Quality Control: The change directly affects the quality control process for products 3226 and 3022, as a new analytical method must be developed for the new instrument. As the production of product 3226 is regular occurrance with significant financial margins, it is considered necessary to validate the method specific for that product before use, according to current validation guidelines (ICH Q2(R2) and internal SOPs). Validation of the method specific for product 3022 will only be performed if customer requires it and will cover the cost. Although there are no changes to the product specification or shelf-life, the analytical method must be demonstrated to be equivalent or superior to the existing method with FACS CAlibur (CA554). All method development and validation documentation must be retained in the document control system.      Logistics: Implementation of the new instrument and method will require new reagents and consumables.     ·        MACSQuant® Calibration Beads (catalogue number: 130-093-607).    ·        MACSQuant® Washing Solution (catalogue number: 130-092-749).    ·        MACSQuant®/MACSima™ Storage Solution (catalogue number: 130-092-748).     ·        MACSQuant® Running Buffer Concentrate (16×) (catalogue number: 130-111-747).     ·        Flow Cytometry tubes 5 ml (Sarstedt, catalogue number: 55.1579)     ·        Chill 96 Rack (Miltenyi Biotec, catalogue number: 130-094-459)     These must be registered in the NOVA ERP system. All suppliers of new reagents must be qualified or re-evaluated according to Curida’s supplier management procedures (CA555). For each new material, evaluate if a  product specification should be created or updated according to SOP5010 (CA556).    Validation Status: The current QC method using the FACS Calibur is not formally validated for either product, which reduces the risk associated with replacement. Nevertheless, the new method must be validated for product 3226 before use in routine QC testing to ensure its robustness. (CA554) The validation plan will follow both internal SOPs and ICH Q2(R2) standards. Validation must be approved by QA, and the final report must be reviewed and stored within the QMS. The validated method must include the product-specific testing requirements. Validation of method specific for product 3022 will be performed if required by the customer who will cover the additional cost. Customer will be informed of this alternative when receiving change notification (CA561).    Qualification status: As the new instrument is rented and located outside of Curida’s direct facility, it is not formally qualified per Curida Diatec's equipment qualification procedures. Instead of full IQ/OQ/PQ, instrument suitability will be ensured through documented system suitability checks performed prior to each use. The SOP for the instrument must define these checks in detail, and records must be maintained. (CA553) The agreement with the third-party owner will specify maintenance schedules, calibration verification, and cleaning responsibilities. (CA552)      HSE: The reagents used in the new method are standard and are already handled at Curida Diatec, posing no new HSE risks. However, since the work will be performed at an external laboratory, HSE training must be provided to Curida personnel accessing the site. This includes basic safety procedures, emergency contacts, and chemical handling protocols. (CA558) Additionally, MSDS for all reagents must be available and reviewed (CA557), and Curida must ensure that the external lab meets minimal safety infrastructure requirements. These measures should be confirmed in a HSE checklist (CA551).    Training: Training is critical to ensuring successful implementation of the new method and the proper use of the new instrument. All internal staff involved in QC testing must receive training on the instrument SOP, sample preparation updates, and the new method.(CA559) The training must be documented. Additionally, personnel from the third-party facility responsible for maintenance or system support must be able to provide training documentation for the system (CA559)      Documentation: To reflect the instrument change, the current SOP for FACS Calibur (SOP4013) will be discontinued, and relevant updates will be made to SOP1046 (sample preparation). During the feasibility study two main changes to the sample prep were identified, final buffer for the samples should be PBS (readily available and physiologically stable buffer) instead of FACS flow, and sample size is reduced from 300 µl to 150-200 µl as new instrument needs less sample and can use a 96-well plate. A new SOP will be created for the selected instrument, detailing instrument setup, data acquisition, system suitability, cleaning, and maintenance (CA553). The method validation protocol and report must also be included in the document management system. (CA554)    Customers: Because this change affects the analytical method used for commercial products, all relevant customers must be notified. A formal communication will be sent to customers explaining the reason for the change, the outcome of method comparability or validation studies, and any potential impact on reporting or product testing. This is documented in a change notification (CA560).    Compliance:  The change will be implemented in full accordance with Curida Diatec’s QMS and ISO 9001 requirements. A documented change control is initiated and must include risk assessment, method validation, supplier qualification, SOP updates, and personnel training. QA oversight will be maintained throughout, and implementation will only occur after formal approval.
Planned implementation data - comment: All actions must be completed prior to use of method in production.

Change Title: Streamlining the training records module in BizzMine.
Change from:: A multi-step process where each part of the three level (theoretical, practical, and independent) training plan needs to be electronically signed by trainer individually. Training plan need trainer and trainee at each of the steps Cancel training is done by Admin level.
Change to: One step encompassing all levels of training with trainer(s) electronically signing for all training performed in one action. Added option to cancel training by supervisor and trainer.
Justification for Change: The process of updating the training module requires re-validation, therefore, it is classified as a major change. The multiple steps involved in every training plan have been impractical. Practical and independent training sessions are typically conducted in the lab area, where immediate access to electronic training record signing is not always available. This could create a risk of training records remaining incomplete after the session. Additionally, theoretical and practical training levels are often executed simultaneously by the same trainer. This update simplifies the training record process by reducing the number of steps required for completion, ensuring compliance and maintaining good documentation practices. This update is based on user feedback following the change review of CMID9, anchored in CAPA89.     The ability to cancel training can be beneficial when an employee changes positions and no longer requires specific training or when an operation linked to a training module is discontinued. The responsibility for canceling training should rest with the technical supervisor and trainer, as they have better oversight of training requirements. While there is a risk of accidental cancellation, this risk is considered low and mitigated by the requirement of an electronic signature in BizzMine.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Facility/Utility/Equipment/Logistic/HSA:      NA.  Production/Quality Control:                         There is no changes to the training plans for production. Any new training for procedures will be documented in a similar manner as before.                                            Training and Personnel:                              Team must be notified of updated training plan module. Change action 532. Documentation:                                           Documentation of training uses the same layout as before. The training can also be added when all three levels are completed, with relevant references and dates. SOP5047 Training Management" does not require an update from this change as the information regarding the training module remains valid for the new version. Customers/Compliance:                              This change does not impose any critical changes to products or their manufacturing process. No customers need to be informed about this change. It complies with our current ISO9001:2015 standard.  eQMS:                                                         The new training module must be tested and re-validated before use. This will result in downtime during the validation where no new training plans can be recorded. Change action 533.   Risk assessment: There is still a risk that training plans are not finalized when the training is given, but this is considered reduced due to the reduced number of steps. This is considered a minor risk as both trainer and trainee will receive email and BizzMine notifications. If the validation of the new training module does not succeed it could impose longer downtime for training module. This is considered a minor risk. Additionally, most training is documented in worksheets at time of execution giving traceability for later logging of the training in BizzMine eQMS. Since multiple levels of training can be registered at the same time it might not be clear which level of training a person is at if this is not documented to the training records immediately. This risk is mitigated by having active tasks and notifications."
Planned implementation data - comment: NA

Change Title: Extend internally set expiration date for Albumin solution human (A9080-10 mL).
Change from:: Acceptable Expiry Date “Minimum 1 year after received material” according to Specification of Incoming Good SP4170 for A9080 Albumin solution human from 15.FEB.2021 (Ref. Attachment 1).
Change to: Expiry date 3 years after received material.
Justification for Change: Human Serum Albumin, 30% Solution, Sigma cat. A9080 is used as the coating reagent for Human Serum Albumin specificity test by ELISA. This is a QC test performed exclusively for the clone 2D2 (product number 8530) for Abbott Diagnostics Technologies AS. The supplier of this product does not assign an expiration date or recommended retest date, but instead promote regular testing and inspection of the product. The product is however subject to a one year warranty from the date of shipment. (Ref. Attachment 2). Curida Diatec has operated with an internal expiry date of one year after material is received according to Specification of Incoming Good SP4170 for A9080 Albumin solution human from 15.FEB.2021 (Ref. Attachment 1). When placing an order for new HSA due to internally set expiry date, Curida Diatec was informed that there were production issues and new lot would not be available until earlyest end of march 2025, latest march 2026. As without this material QC testing of product 8520 2D2 can not be completed and final product released. customer was contacted with several options (wait and hope for delivery of HSA in time, change the HSA used completely or if available, supply Curida Diatec with some HSA to use. From correspondence with customer (meetings and email with Tom Christian Berg, Department manager for Technical production Synthesis, Solutions and Improvements at Abbott Diagnostics Technologies AS ), it was determined that the HSA coat in house was from the same batch (lot SLCL4194) as Curida Diatecs, however Abbott Diagnostics Technologies AS has an internal expiry of three years without observing issues with functionality. Abbott Diagnostics Technologies AS expressed their wish that Curdia Diatec extends the shelf of HSA coating (Ref. Attachment 3). To document the functionality of the coat, internal testing was done on two sets of older HSA coating (same lot SLCL4194) available in house with a recently completed 2D2 product that had already passed QC (E6527) and successfully been used as a control sample (E6620). To approve a QC ELISA run, the following acceptance criteria must be met:- OD of control must be > 0.5 more than blank.- OD of sample must be > 0.5 more than blank.Duplicates must be comparable. Both coats in the internal test gave clear positive results for samples and negatives for blank, as well as comparable quadruplicates (E6648. performed on 20.02.2025, Ref. Attachment 4). The results were also compared with five successful QC ELISA runs performed for earlier approved products and the OD measurements are comparable between the ELISAs as shown in table under.  ELISA run no Batch OD measurement sample OD measurement blank Time passed between HSA coating was   received and used. E6343  B8543  2,6 / 2,5  0,07 / 0,07  <1 year    B8685  2,6 / 2,4  0,07 / 0,07  <1 year            E6503  B8929      1,2 / 1,3  0,05 / 0,05  <1 year    B8782  1,1 / 1,5  0,05 / 0,05  <1 year            E6430  B8782  2,2 / 1,9  0,1 / 0,1  <1 year    B8685  2,1 / 1,7  0,1 / 0,1  <1 year            E6527  B8782  3,1 / 3,1  0,08 / 0,1  <1 year    B8975  3,0 / 3,1  0,08 / 0,1  <1 year            E6620  B8975  2,4 / 2,4  0,08 / 0,08  <1 year    B9105  2,2 / 2,1  0,08 / 0,08  <1 year    B9106  2,4 / 2,1  0,08 / 0,08  <1 year            E6648  B8975  2,1 / 2,3 / 2,2 / 2,1  0,07 / 0,06 / 0,06 / 0,06  3 years. From 11/2022    B8975  2,1 / 1,9 / 1,8 / 1,8  0,06 / 0,07 / 0,06 / 0,06  2 years. From 04/2023  All OD measurements are performed for samples and blanks in duplicates for QC ELISA, or quadruplicates for the internal testing of older HSA coats. Coat used for all was A9080, lot no: SLCL4194.  As a conclusion: The customer of 2D2 operates with a three year shelf life for HSA coating without issues, and internal testing confirms that the reagent is working as expected and there is no noticeable decrease in performance compared to ELISA assays where newer coats are used.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Risk assessment: - There appears to be no risk of this change impacting the finished product. If the quality of the coating were to reduce within the new set expiry date, it would be discovered by the weak or lack of results for the control sample run together with the product to be approved on the QC ELISA. Furthermore, customer operates with  3 years expiry when using the coat, without issues which further confirms that there is no risk to store the material for an additional year.  - No risk to compliance or validation is identified.
