Change Title: Implement Cedex Bioanalyzer kit for in-process control of mouse IgG titre in cell culture supernatants in Production at the Diagnostic site.
Change from:: ELISA method (SOP1007) is used for determining IgG antibody concentration as an in-process control in upstream production at the Diagnostics site.
Change to: Ready-to-use assay on the Cedex Bioanalyzer (CMID38) will be used for antibody concentration determination as an in-process control in upstream production. The first assay implemented will be for mouse IgG. The ELISA method will be continued for many of Curida Diatec’s products and will also serve as a backup solution for mouse IgG analysis.
Justification for Change: The upstream production process of antibodies in cell cultures requires frequent monitoring of the antibody concentration in the cell culture supernatant. Based on the in-process control results the process settings may be adjusted, the production continued or the production will be terminated. For this purpose, the analytical method SOP 1007 is used. This method utilises the ELISA technique, which requires manual plate preparation and standard and sample dilution. The method doesn’t include positive or negative control, significantly impacting method reliability. The ELISA method has been proven to be unreliable, time-consuming and prone to errors. The last year several deviations have occurred related to the method (refer to NC150,144, 129, 120, 91, 73, 51, 49, 11). Low accuracy and large variation between operators have forced a re-evaluation of the method and a search for an alternative. Ready-to-use assay for mouse IgG antibody determination using the Cedex Bioanalyzer from Roche was tested to evaluate its application for in-process control. Cedex Bioanalyzer is a multifunctional, semiautomatic instrument used for the determination of a wide range of analytes. A feasibility study was conducted, performing a side-by-side comparison of the Cedex Bioanalyzer assay with the current ELISA assay, to assess if the Roche assay can be used as an alternative to the currently used method. The feasibility study evaluated the following parameters: accuracy, precision, specificity, linearity, and method comparison. Results are summarised in the feasibility study report (see attachment 1). The results show that the Roche assay outperformed ELISA on all measured parameters. Therefore, it has been decided to install and qualify the Cedex Bioanalyzer instrument and implement the assay for in-process controls of antibody concentration at the Diagnostic production site.    Note: The instrument installation and qualification (Installation Qualification and Operational Qualification) will be executed in separate change control (Refer to CMID 38).   The positive side of change:-	Ready-to-use assay kit on the Cedex Bioanalyzer can be easily implemented as an in-process control. The MS&T department and Clinical production site already use assays run on Cedex Bioanalyzer. These assays have been used for in-process controls at the Clinical production site, therefore, the organisation already has trained and knowledgeable employees who can support implementation of the method and instrument at the Diagnostic site.-	The method is faster than currently used ELISA method and allows to obtain results in less than 30 minutes. Due to the short analysis time, samples can be tested simultaneously (up to 8 samples in parallel) and more frequently if needed, which can help operators to better control upstream production processes.-	Instrument service and reagents are provided by Roche, which is a well-established supplier with a very good history and reputation. The supplier has already been approved by the Clinical site. No supplier complaint or deviation has been reported since Roche was approved.-	The instrument is calibrated with a reference calibration standard provided by the supplier. The calibration curve stability claim is up to 28 days, therefore less time needs to be spent on calibration compared to ELISA.-	Roche provides ready-to-use mouse IgG samples of known concentration that can be used as controls to check that the instrument/assay works correctly.-	No manual plate preparation, sample preparation or standard dilution is needed.     The negative side of change:-	Currently, over 100 antibodies are manufactured at the Diagnostic site (different clones from different species secreting different antibody isotypes). Several new requests for new test production are received every year. It's not possible to assess method performance for all the antibody products. See risk analysis. -	The ELISA method SOP1007 will be used as a backup in case of Cedex instrument failure, shortage of reagents and when the Roche assay cannot be used for some of the specific clones. Maintaining both assays, Roche and in-house ELISA, will require additional resources for procedure revision and ensuring that needed reagents/consumables are always accessible.-	Method will only be used for mouse IgG determination. The ELISA method will still have to be used to cover all antibody isotypes other than mouse IgG1-3.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Impact assessment: Major change. This change is categorised as major because it will impact the Quality Management System (QMS) by introducing new assays for in-process monitoring of production. There will be a need for changing certain QMS documents (MBRs, SOP, Control plans, etc.), and document training of operators.   Risk assessment:1)	Risk related to calibration:a)	Expired calibrator used for calibrating instrument. Used of an expired calibrator may lead to incorrect results. The risk associated with using an expired calibrator is considered minor. Each calibration is verified using Roche assay control, and control will always be run with samples to minimise the risk (refer to change action 01).b)	Expired calibration curve used. Using an expired calibration curve may lead to incorrect results. The calibration curve stability should be controlled. A control will be run with each sample run to detect the ageing of the calibration curve (refer to change actions 01 and 02).c)	Wrong calibrator values used. Each calibrator set has assigned specific values, which vary from lot to lot. When a new lot of calibrators is used, new calibrator values must be programmed. Using wrong values (using values for different calibrator lots or due to typing errors) will lead to inaccurate results. However, this type of error is easily detectable by using internal control.2)	Risk related to wrong instrument setting.Critical assay parameters such as sample volume, reagent volume, reaction type, reading time and wavelength are part of the supplier's method setting (protocol).  The method is programmed according to the supplier application note (protocol setting) and tested during method validation. Changing the method parameter(s) can decrease method performance and obtain inaccurate results. The unintentional/intentional changes in the method setting can be detected during calibration. If the calibration curve date does not fulfil the internal calibration quality check, the curve will not be accepted, and samples will not be analysed. In addition, internal method control can be run daily (or when instrument is in use if less frequent) to ensure that every run is performed in the correct condition. The risk of unintentional changes can increase if more assays are under testing/implementation and if the instrument is shared with the MS&T department. Therefore, the trained superuser should only program the assay setting. The risk is already mitigated by different access levels as a part of CMID 38.3)	Risk related to the use of reagents:a)	Wrong reagent used. Each of the reagent bottles has a unique barcode which is identified by the instrument software. The software will automatically connect the assay setting with the correct reagents, therefore, the risk of using the wrong reagent is not identified. The instruction on handling reagents will be included in the procedure for this method (refer to change action 02).b)	Contamination of the reagent. Reagent bottles are stored with the cap open in the reagent carousel inside the instrument. If a reagent is contaminated, biologically or chemically, results may be incorrect. Underperformance of the assay should be detected by running internal control (refer to change action 01). c)	Expired reagents. Using expired reagents may lead to incorrect results. Manufacturer claim four weeks onboard stability, which should be controlled as part of the run. An internal control to prevent incorrect results should be included (refer to change actions 01).4)	Risk related to samples/sample preparation:a)	Sample concentration below the quantification limit (LoQ). The sample with a concentration below LOQ is measured with higher inaccuracy/imprecision. No risk was detected in the feasibility study. The results will be flagged as measured below the assay linearity range. The procedure should provide information on handling results measured below the LoQ. Refer to change action 01.b)	Too high sample concentration can result in false low results due to assay antigen excess. The risk is considered medium as the sample will be flagged and can be diluted and rerun. The procedure should provide information on handling results measured above the defined linear range. Refer to change action 02.c)	Sample stability. The impact of storage of antibodies in collected crude samples is unknown, but based on experience over the years antibodies are considered stable when crude is stored at low temperatures. Samples will normally only be stored for shorter periods due to the status as samples for in process control. No action will be performed to mitigate this tisk.d)	Sample matrix. Feasibility study shows the method is robust to obtain reliable results in all currently used cell culture mediums. Harvested samples tested in the feasibility study were tested in 2 different medium types, RPMI1600 and ADCF. For both mediums, accurate results were obtained, and no further action is considered necessary. 5)	Data integrity. Obtained results are used to monitor the production process. False data may lead to incorrect/unintended control actions to the production process. Therefore, data must be protected from the unintended modification. The step to ensure data integrity will be assessed in change control for Cedex implementation. The risk is already mitigated by creating a user account with different access levels as a part of CMID38.A defined data report should be designed and attached to relevant production worksheets. 6)	Method specificity. In the feasibility study 1 and 2, the method specificity was tested using two different clones for different subclass (Igg1, IgG2a and IgG2b, IgG3). (see attachment 1 and attachment 3). The comparable accuracy and precision was observed for all tested clones. However, more than 100 different IgG clones, of 5 different subclasses (IgG1, IgG2a, IgG2b, IgG3, IgG4) are produced at Diatec, and several test productions of new clones are requested by customers each year. It will not be possible to test specificity against all of them to prove that method performance is the same for all the products.What is more, method specificity can vary between different antibody subclass.The supplied Anti-IgG antiserum enables detection of Mouse IgG subclasses IgG1, IgG2a, and IgG2b. Nevertheless, the calibrator CAL F (used as a part of the reagent kit) is optimised for accurate test calibration for the determination of IgG1. The affinity of the antiserum may be slightly different for other subclasses (IgG2a, IgG2b). See attachment 2. Therefore, results for the other subclasses may be less accurate but still within the expected criteria. Refer to change action 01.
Planned implementation data - comment: Change was planned to be implemented on 30.APR.2024, but was delayed by approx.8months.

Change Title: Implement Cedex Bioanalyzer for in-process control of antibody concentration (titer) and glucose content in cell culture supernatants in Production at the Diagnostic site.
Change from:: An ELISA technique (method SOP 1007) is used for antibody concentration (titer), and a lateral flow technique (dip stick) for glucose concentration (SOP 1050) are used for in-process control analysis of cell culture supernatants.
Change to: Implement Cedex Bioanalyzer (instrument no DIA410) for in-process control of antibody concentration (titer) and glucose content in cell culture supernatants in Production at the Diagnostic site. Other analytical methods available on the instrument will be evaluated in the future according to needs.
Justification for Change: Impact assessment: Major change This change is categorized as major because it will impact the Quality Management System (QMS) by introducing a new instrument and new assays. There will be a need for installation and qualification, preparation of SOP and other QMS documents, and training of personnel. Justification: Cedex Bioanalyzer is a multifunctional, semiautomatic tool used to determine a wide range of analytes. Our current in-process control (IPC) utilising ELISA (for antibodies determination) and later flow (glucose determination) has proven unreliable, time-consuming and resource-demanding. Low accuracy, large variation between operators and several deviations reported in the last years have forced the company to search for alternative solutions.  An equal Cedex Bioanalyzer instrument has been used for in-process controls in Production at the Clinical site. That instrument was qualified and a method SOP was established (ref QC-012-SOP). Installation Qualification and Operational Qualification of DIA410 as well as SOP for use, can be built on this. The DIA410 instrument for Diagnostics has been used by the MS&T department and will be installed in the production area. Both QC operators at Clinical and Subject Matter Experts in MS&T are available for supporting the installation, analysis set up, qualification and training. Several ready-to-use assays can be implemented at this analyser to support the production processes. Which assays will be decied based on cost, time, availability and readiness by the organization. Any new assay inplementation shall be handled in separate change controls.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: The positive side of the change: -    The instrument is at hand for the Diagnostics site, without any investments required. A service of the instrument has recently been performed after moving it from the location at Forskningsparken. No concerns were raised during service.   -   Manhours required to run an assay is low. Most assays on the platform can deliver results in less than 20 minutes. Sample preparation is normally not required.       -   A feasibility study performed with mouse IgG1 assay showed better precision and accuracy when compared to the current ELISA assay. The expectation is that the number of non-conformances therefore will be reduced, saving time and effort of personnel. -   The instrument is a user-friendly multifunction instrument and will allow fast implementation of several types of assays. -    Assays can be used for both in-process control and potentially Quality Control. After IQ and OQ, each of the assays will be implemented via individual change control, including Performance Qualification. -    Bench top instrument, with no requirement for connection to water sources or sewage system. -     Instrument service and reagents are provided by Roche, which is a well-established supplier with a very good history and reputation. The Clinical site has already approved the supplier, and no supplier complaint or deviation has been reported to that supplier since it was approved.   The negative side of the change: -    Cost of kit reagents for some assays is higher than currently used reagents (for example, for IgG antibody concentration determination). -    Additional cost of consumables such as cuvettes or wash solutions.   Impacted area and risk assessment: 1. Facility: facility requirements will be defined and addressed during installation qualification. The topic will be addressed in Change Action 202 (01). 2. HSE – hazard to human health and safety and the environment should be assessed, and appropriate steps should be taken.  a) The main hazard to human health and safety are considered: - Risk related to handling biological material – as most production at the Diagnostic site is related to biological material, all operators are trained in safely handling biological materials, the topic is covered by HSE training performed at the Diagnostic site. Therefore, there is no additional risk to personnel handling biological material. - Risks related to handling wash solution (Sodium hypochlorite (2.5% Cl₂) a weak basic wash used to clean the sample/reagent probe.  Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS) must be available according to SOP 5017. Risk is considered low, and safety instructions should be included in the procedure for the instrument. HMS aspects will be partially covered by operator training and instrument SOP. Refer to change action 203 (02). - Risk related to the operating instrument – sample/reagent probe can punch operator skin if some operations are performed when the instrument is running analysis. The operator should be notified regarding the risk during operator training. Safety instructions should be included in the procedure for the instrument. HMS aspects will be partially covered by operator training and instrument SOP. Refer to change action 203 (02). b) The main hazard to the environment: - The risk associated with waste management, including using biological samples and reaction cuvettes - all material used will be discarded according to the local procedure for waste management (SOP 4006). The liquid waste after probe wash is considered to carry low biological risk due to the over 1000 dilution rate of pipetted sample volume with the wash solution. Wash solution represent low risk to environment due to low concentration. 3. Suppliers – Instrument service, consumables, and assay reagents are provided by Roche, the wash solution is provided by VWR.  Roche is a well-established supplier with a very good history and reputation. The Clinical site already approved the supplier, and no supplier complaint or deviation has been reported to that supplier since it was approved. The supplier will have to be classified and qualified at the Diagnostic site as well. Refer to change action 205 (04). VWR is an approved supplier. 4. QMS – Installation and implementation of the instrument should comply with the local procedure for Equipment (ref SOP 5043). The instrument implementation form should be prepared and approved for the instrument.  The Instrument implementation form (DOC 5043.2) covers the following aspects of the QMS: instrument labelling, maintenance and calibration, documentation, and training. The topic will be addressed in change action 204 (03). 5. Personnel – Sufficient training should be provided for production operators. The training module should be established according to the local procedure for training handling (SOP 5047). The training records should be prepared and approved for all trained operators. The topic will be addressed in change actions 206 (05) and 207 (06). 6. Logistic – all consumables must be defined and categorised according to the local procedure for incoming material control (SOP 5010). The specification should include a quality control approach and release criteria. The incoming material control should be established if needed, and else any experience with the reagents used at the Clinical site should be considered relevant and taken into account. The topic will be addressed in change action 208 (07).  7. Qualification – The instrument will be used in the in-process control of the upstream production process. Therefore, a complete instrument qualification should be performed according to the local procedure for Qualification and Validation activities (SOP 5018). The qualification should be divided into three steps, of which the first two are addressed in this change:  a) Installation Qualification: assess localisation and access to utilities for the instrument. see Change Action 202 (01). b) Operational Qualification: assess the operation of critical parameters of the instrument such as accuracy and precision of sample/reagent probe or cooling system. see Change Action 202 (01). c) Performance Qualification: PQ should be executed individually for each assay (performed according to individual change for each assay implementation). The first change will be executed for the implementation of an immunoassay for mouse IgG concentration determination (refer to CMID39). 8. IT security The assay setting (protocol) consists of several assay parameters, such as reagent/samples volume, reaction type, wavelength etc. Unintentional changes to the setting could lead to a significant impact on assay validation status and obtaining unreliable results. The instrument has four levels of access: system administrator, service engineer, superuser and operator with distinct differences in accessibility to change of parameter settings. Access to the instrument (settings) should be password protected. User accounts with different access levels should be established. Refer to change action 204 (03). 9. Data integrity False data may lead to incorrect/unintended control actions in the production process. Therefore, data must be protected from the unintended modifications. Securing correct access level for operators will mitigate the risk. Refer to change action 204 (03). Training of operators should include training in such requirements for data integrity, refer to change action 207 (06).
Planned implementation data - comment: NA

Change Title: Include manual washing in ELISA procedures as a backup for the ELISA washer.
Change from:: ELISA procedures (SOP1007/1014/1025) states that the wash steps during the analysis must be performed by the Wellwash Versa.
Change to: Include manual wash of ELISA plates to our ELISA procedures (SOP1007/1014/1025) to be used as backup for the Wellwash Versa.
Justification for Change: Justification for change: On the 26.JUL.2023 the ELISA washer Wellwash Versa did not function. The keypad to the Wellwash Versa was not working properly and the instrument can not be used until service is performed. In our SOP`s (SOP1007/1014/1025) it is stated that the washing steps i the Elisa procedures has to be done with the Wellwash Versa. In order to perform the in-process monitoring of ongoing productions while the Wellwash Versa is out of order, it is considered feasible to include the possibility to perform the wash step in the ELISA analysis by manual washing, using either a multi pipette or a wash bottle. This option is already available as a part of other ELISA procedures such as SOP1039 v4.0. and SOP1097 v4.0.   Impact assessment: 1.Facility, equipment, HSE, Suppliers, Logistics: The same buffer as is used for the Wellwash Vera (PBS-Tween), will be used for the wash step. Equipment needed (pipettes and/or wash bottle) are already implemented and in use. Therefore, this change has no impact on the facility, equipment, HSE, suppliers or logistics.  3. Qualification/Validation: As the analysis method itself is not changed, this change does not impact the Qualification/Validation status of instruments or method. 4. Production: Buffer used for washing is the same, time for executing the washing process is slightly longer, but still within defined time limits in the procedure. Therefore this has no impact. Wrong concentration measured due to cross contamination, can increase with manual wash which in turn can impact production yield. However this is considered low risk as discussed in the risk assessment below. 5. Quality Management System (QMS): SOPs 1007, 1014 and 1025 must be updated with the new washing alternative. All personnel must be trained in the new procedure.  Risk assessment and mitigating actions: Risk of cross contamination: If wells are not washed thoroughly enough, it might result in cross-contamination and false results. It is therefore important to wash the wells thoroughly, suggested to wash the wells at least 4 times in PBS-tween (same procedure as in SOP1039 and SOP1097). Manual wash can be performed either by using a multi pipette or a wash bottle. By using a multi pipette the volume used for the wash can be controlled to correspond to the volume used by the Wellwash Versa, but exact volume is not critical, as long as the wells are filled up. By using a multi pipette it will take significantly longer to performed the wash step. Using a wash bottle will take significantly shorter time than using a multi pipette. By using a wash bottle exact volume cannot be controlled, but as long as the wells are filled it is sufficient. In case of an overfilling of the wells by using a wash bottle the probability for a cross contamination to the nearby wells is considered low as the solutions in the wells are already removed prior to washing, and as reaction time during the wash step is short. Therefore either pipette or wash bottle can be used.  SOP1017/1014/1025 must be updated (Change action 185). All personnel to perform in-process ELISA analysis with manual wash step will need retraining in the new procedure prior to performing ELISA (Change action 184).
Impacted areas of the organization and r: This change can be implemented after change actions 183 and 184 are completed. SOP updates (Change action 185) do not need to be completed before this change can be initiated. Instead a protocol has to be generated and approved for use (Change action 183) and all operators to perform ELISA must be trained in the protocol and training must be documented. The protocol must be available and visible to all in the ELISA area of the analytical lab. If the update to the SOPs is the same as the procedure in the protocol, additional training does not need to be performed (other than Read and understand), but all personnel must be informed that the protocol is not in use anymore, as the SOPs have been updated (Change action 188). Due to the upgrade of the non-conformance module Bizzmine, it was not possible to initiate a non-conformance report regarding the equipment malfunction in week 31 - 33. A non conformance regarding this incident will be created later (Change action 182). This non-conformance has ID181.
Planned implementation data - comment: This change can be implemented after change actions 183 and 184 are completed.

Change Title: Changes in Organisation structure, roles, and responsibilities
Change from:: Two organisation charts, one for each production site, are established. The organisation chart for Diagnostic is documented in DOC5016.1. The majority of current valid QMS documents have assigned responsibility to titles or roles that are very specifically defined, which leads to problems when the organisation is changing, new roles are introduced, and some titles no longer exist.
Change to: One organisation chart showing the functions of the whole company of Curida Diatec AS. As a business decision, the organisational structure of Curida Diatec has changed. The change has introduced a new level of managers, Head of Operations, Head of Quality, Head of Manufacturing Science and Technologies (MS&T), and Head of Business Development, who the department managers at both the clinical and diagnostic part of Curida Diatec will report to. Attachment 1 shows the chart of the new organisation structure as presented at all employee meeting 02.MAR.2023 (chart to be confirmed or updated as needed as part of action 252). The departments serve both clinical and diagnostics units, but their function and responsibilities on the diagnostic unit will be managed in the Diagnostics’ QMS. All Job descriptions shall be aligned with current roles, defined responsibilities, authorities, and reporting relationships.  The heads of departments will report to the CEO. It has also been introduced a new role in the Curida group, the Chief Operating Officer (COO). The Head of Quality and Head of Operations in Curida Diatec will have a dotted line reporting relationship to the COO. The Research and development (R&D) department has been renamed to Manufacturing Science and Technologies (MS&T). Titles that no longer exist:- General Manager (Replaced/equivalent with CEO)- Site Manager (Removed)- Quality Assurance Director (Replaced/equivalent with Quality Assurance Manager)- Sales Manager (Replaced with Business development manager)- R&D Manager (Replaced with Head of MS&T) All valid QMS documents shall refer to the correct role/title/department when assigning responsibilities. The roles with assigned responsibility will be defined to be flexible and robust for minor changes in the organisation, though the terminology must be unambiguous. The assigned responsibilities in these documents shall be aligned with the job descriptions, workflow, workload, and current ISO 9001 standard.
Justification for Change: As a business decision, the organisational structure of Curida Diatec has changed. There have been introduced new roles, titles, and departments. The organisation chart includes both the clinical and diagnostic site, and the new level of managers, head of departments, will have responsibilities covering both. Many SOPs refer to departments and roles/titles and need to change accordingly. The QMS should be updated to reflect the current organisation and be flexible and robust for minor changes in titles. Advantages: A more unified and efficient organisation. The QMS will reflect current roles and responsibilities and will be more flexible for minor changes in the structure of the organisation. Disadvantages: Changes to documentation must be done and is time consuming.
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Describe risk and mitigating action: Risk1: Responsibilities are inconsistent across documents, role descriptions, and job descriptions.Mitigating action: Job descriptions and QMS documentation will be reviewed and updated to comply with this change. Risk 2: Personnel not aware of their responsibilities. When roles or titles are listed in a document, the terminology used should be unambiguous, leaving no doubt about to whom the assigned responsibilities belong.Migitating action: All documents must be read and understood by relevant personnel. When new documents are authorized or personnel get new roles, they shall be made aware of their responsibilities through reading the document and be provided training where applicable in accordance with SOP5047 Handling of training.  Risk 3: Responsibility assigned to a title that no longer exists.Migitating action: All the instructional documentation shall be reviewed to detect where there are referred to titles, roles or departments that no longer exist. The documentation shall be updated and, where possible, be made more flexible for minor changes in the organisation. Documents to be updated are listed in attachment 2.  Risk 4: Responsibility no longer assigned when updating the document.Migitating action: Documents are written, reviewed, and approved in accordance with SOP5042 to assure all responsibilities needed are defined. A matrix listing the current documents and their responsibilities have been made (ref attachment 2) to assure they either is accounted for or removed/changed with justification.  Risk 5: Responsibilities assigned to personnel not able to perform their task. Mitigating action: Responsibilities should be assigned at the lowest possible level, and where possible, not be assigned to single people. If single person roles must have a defined responsibility, the organisation must ensure that this role always is available in the organisation to perform that task and has a deputy in case of absence.
Planned implementation data - comment: Change is valid when the organization chart is updated no later than the end of January 2024. All job descriptions have to be assessed and updated if needed within Q1 2024. All documents updated have to be finalised by the end of 2024. Full change implementation should be executed by the end of 2024.

Change Title: Exchange AcuSyst-Xcellerator present in AcuSyst room (DIA277) to one from GMP-site
Change from:: Production of monoclonals antibodies at the Diagnostic site is currently conducted in CL1000 bioreactor (low-scale production) and AcuSyst Maximiser (high-scale production). According to CMID8 AcuSyst Xcellerator is being implemented and qualified.
Change to: In addition to currently used bioreactors, CL1000 and AcuSyst Maximiser, Acusyst Xcellerator will be implemented into the ISO site to increase production capacity. The current AcuSyst Xcellerator being implemented (CMID8) will be discontinued and exchanged with another Xcellerator from GMP site (EQ1033 or EQ1034) which both have a demonstrated ability to produce according to standard procedures.
Justification for Change: Justification: The Acusyst Xcellerator (Xcellerator) is an advanced mammalian cell culture system for large-scale efficient and economical production of cell-secreted proteins. This automated system provides an optimal environment for cell growth and production. The Xcellerator is comprised of: - Xcellerator cell culture instrument, containing an incubator and refrigerator sections. - Built-in control computer running ACUSOFT-Xcellerator software - Two independent, disposable flowpaths that may be operated simultaneously - Two medium warmer modules   Xcellerator is the second-hand instrument used at the GMP site of Diatec Monoclonals for pharmaceutical production. Due to the termination of the agreement with the customer, the GMP site discontinued using the instrument and offered it to the ISO site free of charge. GMP site has 3 Xcellerator instruments, two implemented into GMP quality system and used for successful PPQ of lilotomab and one used for R&D work. When CMID8 was initiated and Xcellerator was moved to Diagnostic site, the instrument available for moving was DIA277, the R&D instrument located in analytical lab at GMP site. As the GMP site will now be moving from FP to Fornebu, both GMP Xcellerator instruments have been decommissioned from GMP QMS and will not be used further for GMP production. The GMP instruments have a demonstrated ability to produce good quality product consistently, and have been maintained according to the strict quality standards of GMP. Therefore it is considered more beneficial to exchange DIA277 for either of the GMP instruments (EQ1033 or EQ1034), where it is believed they will be more stable than DIA277 that previously has had some issues and has not been maintained to the same standard as EQ1033 or EQ1034
Impacted areas of the organization and r: DIA277 has been implemented to Diagnostic site, however due to production trouble, qualification of the instrument has not been completed as of July 2023. When this CR has been approved, CMID8 and all the pending actions will be closed with reference to this CR (Action 161). Decommissioning of DIA277, including placing instrument as inactive in instrument list, removing label, archiving instrument binder, and removing instrument from site, is handled as according to action 162.   Positive impact  The Xcellerator is a similar instrument to the Acusyst Maximiser bioreactors (Maximiser) currently used in production on the ISO site but has higher production capabilities. It operates with the same technologies and hollow fiber system as Maximisers, with either 6 or 10 hollow fiber cartridges instead of 2 cartridges of a maximisers cultureware, identical in size and composition to the Xcellerator. Implementing Xcellerator can help increase the production productivity up to 5 fold, and clones already produced on the Maximiser will most likely produce well in Xcellerator. Another advantage is the integrated software that can be connected to a network. Integrated software will allow for remote monitoring and can be connected to an external monitoring system (Sirius system), something the Maximisers do not have. Using the Xcellerator will increase the detection rate of any issues with the production and the possibility of correcting them before it results in a run termination. The same approved supplier will provide services for Maximisers and Xcellerators, Cell Culture Company (C3).  Instrument implementation and test production will be supported by operators that worked at the GMP site, and are considered experts on operating the instrument. Therefore, implementing the instrument and initiating a test production should be executed efficiently and without many resources required. The instrument was given free of charge, therefore cost of the implementation is limited only to the working hours of the employees involved in the process.    Negative impact: Due to high operational costs, like the cost of consumables and needed working hours, The Xcellerator is only suitable for a large order of antibodies. Moreover, this instrument cannot be suitable for all types of clones used in production at the Diagnostic site. Using the Maximiser instrument for test production using a new clone will be recommended. The Xcellerator instrument will be located in room 106 (AS production room) in the Diagnostic production facility. Room 106 is currently used for large-scale production using Maximiser bioreactors. When the Xcellerator is placed in this room, adding more bioreactors in this localization will not be possible. If the demand for a product continues, the production facility will have to be redesigned/ extended or the Maximiser instruments should be replaced with instruments of higher capacity.  Like the Maximisers, the Xcellerator requires a constant medium supply. The instrument will be connected to medium tanks, stored in a cold room, and supplied via tubings. As utilizing just one of the Xcellerator flowpaths uses more medium than a Maximisers run, additional tank(s) should be available at all times. The space in the cold room will be fully utilized, which will put more pressure on the cold room storage capacity at the ISO production site. If the demand for a product increases and multiple AcuSyst instruments run simultaneously, the production facility must be redesigned or extended.   Risk assessment Implementing the Xcellerator bioreactor will impact several areas of the Diagnostic site organization. The main impact is assessed and summarised below: 1. Facility: facility requirements will be defined and addressed during installation qualification. Instrument and medium tanks needed for production will challenge cold storage capacity at the ISO production site. The topic will be addressed in Change action 163. 2. HSE – hazard to human health and safety and the environment should be assessed, and appropriate steps should be taken.  a) The main hazard to human health and safety are considered: - Risk related to handling biological material – as most production at the Diagnostic site is related to biological material, all operators are trained in safely handling biological materials, the topic is covered by HSE training performed at the ISO site. Therefore there is no additional risk to personnel related to handling biological material. - Risks related to handling CO2 gas – all operators are trained in handling CO2 gas, the topic is covered by HSE training all operators must complete. Therefore no additional risk to personnel related to this change. - HMS aspects will be partially covered by operator training and instrument SOP - Training modules were established during CMID8, but should be re-evaluated. The topic will be addressed in Change actions 164 and 165. Training will address the risk related to weight lifting and the risk of electric shock. b) The main hazard to the environment: - The risk associated to waste management, including used biological medium and bioreactors consumables (flow path)  –  all consumable parts used in production will be utilized according to the local procedure for waste management (SOP4006). - The risk associated with handling biological material and the risk of biological contamination – Xcellerator will not increase the risk of biological contaminants in the production facility. The Xcellerator bioreactor is a closed system, which highly limits the production facility's risk of biological contamination. After cell inoculation, there is no higher risk of contamination from an Xcellerator than already used Maximiser instruments. The Xcellerator requires a higher amount of cells to inoculate, about 5x the amount needed for a Maximiser. With increased handling of the cell culture, the chances of infection are higher, with the risk of spreading within the facility. However, the operators are highly trained in expanding cells, screening for infection during the expansion phase, and are validated annually on their aseptic technique. In addition, environment monitoring is performed in the production facility according to a local procedure (SOP 1076). The result of the monitoring conducted during PQ will be inspected if instrument implementation did cause an increased risk of biological contamination in the production area. The topic will be addressed in change action 166. 3. Suppliers –  Cell Culter Company (C3) will provide the instrument service and calibration, in addition to the bioreactor and its consumables. C3 is already a qualified and approved supplier for the ISO production site. However, the agreement with the supplier should be revised to evaluate from a production and business perspective (evaluate service and consumables delivery). The topic will be addressed in change action 167. 4. QMS – Implementation of the instrument should comply with the local procedure for Equipment (ref SOP 5043). The instrument implementation form should be prepared and approved for Xcellerator and all other supporting instruments used in the test production process.  The Instrument implementation form (DOC 5043.2) covers the following aspects of the QMS: instrument labeling, maintenance and calibration, documentation, and training. The topic will be addressed in change action 168, 169 and 170. 5. Personnel – Sufficient training should be provided for production operators. The training module should be established according to the local procedure for training handling (SOP 5047). The training records should be prepared and approved for all trained operators. The topic will be addressed in change actions 164 and 165. 6. Logistic – all consumables must be defined and categorized according to the local procedure for incoming material control (SOP 5010). The specification should include a quality control approach and release criteria. The topic will be addressed in change action 50. Multiple tubes are needed to run an Xcelerator, YY-tube for medium supply, Y tube for drain, and inoculation manifold. The YY- and Y-tubes can be purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific or produced in-house. The agreement with Thermo Fisher Scientific will not be considered due to the long lead time, and all consumables will be prepared in-house. The inoculation manifold is produced in-house. For in-house production of tubings, document SOP 1082 needs to be updated with the instructions on the assembly of the tubings. This topic will be addressed in action 171. 7. Qualification/Validation – The instrument will be used in the manufacturing process of monoclonals antibodies. Therefore, a complete instrument qualification should be performed according to the local procedure for Qualification and Validation activities (SOP 5018). The qualification should be divided into three steps: The topic will be addressed in change action 172. a) Installation qualification – installation and calibration performed by service representative from C3 Company official instrument suppliers and consumable manufactures. b) operation qualification – installation and calibration performed by service representative from C3 Company official instrument suppliers and consumable manufactures. c) performance qualification – performed as a production run on instrument. 8. Production - Instrument: Xcelerator is similar to Maximisers, however, the software and interphase of the instrument are more advanced, allowing for more monitoring and better control of the instrument. Furthermore, the pumps are similar but not the same. These differences might confuse when running both Maximisers and Xcellerator at the same time by the same operators. This is mitigated by proper training of all operators involved (actions 164 and 165) and the generation of instructional documentation (action 169). - Expansion of cells: inoculation of the Xcellerator requires a high amount of cells (3-5x 109 cells) and, therefore, significantly more T175 flasks (up to 80 flasks) expanded over more days than other bioreactor systems used by Diatec. This can cause incubator capacity problems during expansion for Xcellerators and must be planned accordingly so as not to impact other productions running simultaneously. This issue will be evaluated after PQ has been performed, and the need for the proper solution will be addressed (action 173). - IT security: as Xcellerator is connected to LAN for remote monitoring, unauthorized access to production data and tampering is possible. However, all access outside of Diatec is protected by a firewall administered by Diatecs IT provider, Braathe. Only designated computers (that contain a linked Teamviewer account) can access the Xcellerator monitor and log in from outside the facility.   Based on risk assessment change, actions will be initiated. All aspects related to documentation and validation will be addressed in change actions.
Planned implementation data - comment: Instrument DIA277 will be moved out of AcuSyst room in week 27, and replacing instrument moved into the AcuSyst room. this is to accommodate service scheduled from C3 in week 27. Decommissioning of DIA277 and Implementation of new instruments will be done in parallel PQ will be performed during a run instigated by customer and product will be sold, pending that the instrument PQ is passed and product passess QC.

Change Title: Onboarding training at the Diagnostic site for Clinical site employees.
Department Manager: Anna Huk, Hanne Trømborg, Mira Børstad
Change from:: Curida Diatec consists of two business units with an independent production site, the Clinical site and the Diagnostic site, previously called GMP and ISO, with separate Quality Management Systems.  Employees dedicated to one site (meaning only either Clinical or Diagnostics) are trained only according to the relevant QMS, whereas employees serving both sites have been trained in both QMS to do so.
Change to: Organisation change has been decided and implemented.  A Quality function covers both Quality Assurance for both sites and the Quality control function. Production, Facility, Equipment and Utility (FEU), Manufacturing Science Technology (MS&T, previously named R&D), and Logistics shall cover both sites/ businesses. Employees previously only trained in the Clinical QMS will be trained (onboarded) in the Diagnostic QMS  to gain access to the Diagnostic site’s resources.
Justification for Change: Until March 2023, Diatec Curida consists of two independently operating sites, the Diagnostic site at Technopolis, Fornebu and the Clinical site at Oslo Science Park, Oslo. Both sites have independent Quality Management Systems (QMS). The QMS at the Diagnostic site was established and operated under ISO9001 standards. The Clinical site’s QMS operates under Good Manufacturing Practices requirements. Both sites also maintained separate training processes. Due to the company’s new strategy, the organisation was reorganised as is presented in attachment 1. The main changes are: The facility team will work on both production sites and will overtake some processes from the Production team at the Diagnostic site. QC team will work on both production sites and will overtake some of the processes from the Production team at the Diagnostic site. Both R&D teams will be merged into one MS&T team, which will serve whole organisation Some of the production operators and QA specialists at the Clinical site will be trained to work on the Diagnostic site as well. Both logistic departments will be merged in one Logistics team will work on both production sites and will serve the whole organisation. Some of the Clinical site employees were already on board at the Diagnostic site in 2022 – 2023, when additional help was needed due to the Diagnostics site employee’s absence.     Impacted area: QMS system compliance: the SOP 5047 “Handling of training” requires that every new employee on the Diagnostic site must receive introduction training. The introduction training consists of 7 modules which cover general onboarding, introduction to position, introduction to QMS etc. The introduction training can be adjusted based on personal work experience and education. As the onboarding process at the Clinical site covers several similar topics, given training will be adjusted accordingly: -          If the employee has valid training in this same topic at the Clinical site, training at the Diagnostic site can be completed with reference to GMP training. Employees and its supervisor ensure that training at the Clinical site was given and documented. For example, training in Good Documentation Practice at the Clinical site is valid also for the Diagnostic site. -          If the training is irrelevant to the position, will be voided with a comment: “Not applicable to the position. Example: Training SOP Out of Specification can is not required for the logistic team.   The training will be documented using electronic records in eQMS, BizzMine. The job description will be maintained using Diagnostic or Clinical site template.   HMS: no risk for employees, as the introduction training covers the topic. Every employee who will join the Diagnostic site will receive HMS demonstration as a part of the onboarding process.   Defined risk: the employee will not receive all the needed training. The risk is considered insignificant as the employee supervisor revises and signs all training records. The supervisor will ensure that all introduction training is executed correctly and according to the agreed training matrix (see attachment 2). Job-specific training will be performed and documented fully accordingly to SOP 5047, and therefore, there is no need to be included in that change control. The process of overtaking (by QC, FEU, MS&T) will be addressed in separate individual changes.   The introduction training will be initiated Q2 2023 and will be ongoing for three months from initiation according to actions
Impacted areas of the organization and r: NA
Planned implementation data - comment: The introduction training will be started on 20th of June, and will be ongoing for 3 months, according to SOP 5047 “Handling of training”

Change Title: Use of expired Isotype control for in-process and QC flow cytometry
Change from:: Use of B920 2011 as an isotype control for in process control and QC for Flow Cytometry according to SOP4013 Version 2.0 and SOP1027 Version 2.0
Change to: Temporary use of B920 as an isotype control for in process control and QC for Flow cytometry according to SOP4013 Version 2.0 and SOP1027 Version 2.0 after its given expiry date.
Justification for Change: B920 product has been used previously for all in process control and QC of fluorescence conjugated antibody. However the isotype control expired in december 2005, and has since been used (Ref: NC151). A new commercial isotype control has been ordered from approved supplier BD norge due to this deviation, but no other alternative is available until this is received. Production delay from BD norge caused the expected delivery to be 07.07.2023. Current production 2023-14 will be halted and delivery timeline will have to be moved without the required analyses. Due to the urgency, this temporary change is raised to evaluate the impact of using B920 as a isotype control in these productions. Risk assessment:   1. Production  It should be noted that Curida diatec no longer mark its products with expiry date as the requirements for chemicals does not apply to biologics. B920 product 2011 is a FITC conjugated IgG1 antibody with specificity for KLH. B920 have been stored in cool temperature with continuous monitoring. Additionally, the product is stored in a amber colored vial and kept within a blackbox preventing light from entering to minimize degradation of product (Ref: Attachment 1/2). For fluorophore conjugated antibodies such as B920 this is the preferred method of storage to maintain its integrity. No internal stability studies have been performed for B920, but the expiry date it received is a very conservative predetermined shelflife. in general, monoclonal antibodies have been shown to maintain their functionality for upto 12-26 years under correct storage conditions, including igG isotypes such as this. B920 has been used as a isotype control. Isotype controls are primary antibodies that lack the specificity of the target. It serves as a negative control with the purpose of identifying the non-specific background signal. Especially since SOP1046 does not include any blocking step the isotype control is necessary to show that the signal received from the test antibody is not due to non-specific FC binding of sample components. Non of the technical requirements of SOP1046 include the isotype control. Neither is the signal recorded from the isotype control corrected for in the results. It only gives the operator an additional tool to evaluate the results (SOP1046, Version 2.0, Section 10.1.1). Although no stability studies have been conducted for product 2011, the 14.06.17 a small scale study was performed on product 3226 another fluorophore conjugated antibody (Ref: Attachment 3). The report concluded that of the five batches produced, all still fulfilled the requirements of the product specification. The oldest batch was produced 6 years before this analysis was conducted. The product specification of B920 (Ref: Attachment 4) has requirements for concentration, purity and specificity against blood. Concentration of antibody can be presumed unchanged. The latter has been tested for every flow cytometry analysis performed for products 3226 and 3022 produced after the expiry date. The last QC analysis (Ref: Attachment 5) performed for B8379 yielded a 0% difference in percent positive lymphocytes between unstained and isotype control, which is less than the allowed 2%. The purity however, is difficult to establish since antibody is conjugated and the SDS gel was performed on the unconjugated antibody. Since not any of the Flow cytometry protocols have established requirements for the isotype control and the results received from the isotype control is not accounted in the final results. Therefore it can not affect the approval of products in question. It is however a useful control for both the preparation of samples and to visualise the background signal. Contrary to the expiry date, the evidence and literature seems to support that the stability and functionality of the antibody is preserved. No risk is identified with using B920 until an alternative in available. Change Title: Define transfer volume in SOP0060 for flowpath with two bioreactors
Change from:: SOP0060 version 3.0, pt. 9.16, suggested start up parameters to be set in Process Control:  Transfer vol 70 (ml).
Change to: Suggested start up parameters to be set in Process Control Transfer vol 70 (ml) per bioreactor.
Justification for Change: During starting of AcuSyst Maximizer, one of the start up parameter to define is the desired medium transfer volume between IC and EC. SOP0060 version 3.0 only suggests setting transfer volume of 70 ml during startup. According to the Operations Manual for AcuSyst Maximizer, 70 ml transfer volume is suggested while using one bioreactor. The manual further writes, The default Transfer Vol of 70 ml is sufficient for the first approximately two weeks of active EC Cycling time when using culture with two bioreactors. After this time it may be beneficial to increase the transfer volume in steps of 15-35 ml every 2-3 days until the maximum transfer volume of 140 ml is in use, unless metabolic activity worsens. In such cases, increase the transfer volume more slowly".    The flowpath (cat. # 600068-221) that we use for production, has two bioreactors and the supplier/manual recommended transfer volume is 140 ml for this type of flowpath. The current version of SOP neither defines the limit of transfer volume nor specifies that the suggested transfer volume is for one bioreactor. The default transfer volume (70 ml) remains unchanged throughout the whole production run. The increase in transfer volume is expected to provide better environment for the cells in bioreactor by increasing the volume of medium exchange between EC and IC as well as delay clogging of bioreactor by cells. Clogging of bioreactor is not completely avoidable and obstructs nutrient exchange between IC and EC, ultimately leading to cell death. Since, SOP0060 is prepared based on the supplier's manual, this supplier recommended parameter should not have any impact on the product. Thus, the SOP should be updated to specify transfer volume 70 ml per bioreactor and the recommended routine for having optimal transfer volume (startup volume is set to 70 ml and transfer volume is increased by 15-35 ml every 2-3 days until the maximum volume is 140 ml).  "
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Add info: If metabolic activity worsens, increase the transfer volume more slowly.   Ref. Manual Pg. 6-99
Planned implementation data - comment: The implementation of changi in transfer volume for maximizers is incorporated into CMID48, as SOP0060 is going through major revision. Therefore this change can be voided.

Change Title: Change of protein A column media to Eshmuno A from Merck Millipore
Change from:: ProSep-vA High-Capacity Protein A Chromatography Media, supplied by Merck Millipore, cat no 1131158xx
Change to: Standard material: Eschmuno A High capacity, Merck Millipore Backup material: MabCapture C High Capacity Protein A from Thermo Fisher Scientific and MabSelect SuRe Affinity media from Cytiva. - cat.no is dependent on matrix volume size All three materials are registered identities and can not be confused with any other product from these suppliers.
Justification for Change: Justification for change: Current Protein A chromatography media for purification of antibodies at Curida Diatec Diagnostics facility is to be discontinued by the supplier, ProSep-vA High-Capacity Protein A Chromatography Media, supplied by Merck Millipore, cat no 1131158xx (xx is a number depending on product size). 1L media corresponds to cat no 113115830.  A study of 3 different protein A media was performed, with prosep vA as a reference, according to DEV163_protocol (Attachment 1). Parameters such as yield, relative recovery compared to ProSep vA and purity of the final product were evaluated. The study concluded that all three alternatives performed at least as well as ProSep vA, and Eschmuna A High capacity from Merck Millipore was the preferred alternative due to lowest cost, with Mab Capture C and MabSelect SuRe as good backup solutions, for future use if delivery of Eschmuna A is delayed. This is documented in DEV163-report (attachment 2).
Impacted areas of the organization and r: Impact assessment: Qualification/Validation: no impact to qualification or validation state of FPLC instrument. Currently, the purification process is not qualified. According to ISO9001:2015 standard the process validation is required only when subsequent monitoring or measurements cannot verify the resulting output. The QC analysis for release is conducted for 100 % of the product, therefore, the risk associated with lack of process validation is considered insignificant.  Process/product: The new material testing was performed according to the established purification procedure and protein A 10 ml purification program was used for all testing. All purified products pass quality criteria defined in product specifications for all materials tested. In addition, the number of purified products is the relatively same or bigger compared to the already used material. This indicates that new materials are compatible with the current process, and not additional change to the process itself (like critical parameter: flow rate, pressure) is needed. Documentation: SOP0005 needs to be updated to replace mention of ProSep vA to Eschmuna A, and include MabCapture C and MabSelect SuRe as possibilities. see change action 140. As all on-going projects for IgG production have requirements for ProSep vA in their product specifications (PS), a comment will be made into all on-going PSs referring to this CMID, see change action 142. HSE: low impact on HSE as no dangerous chemicals or components are a part of the matrix.As the resins are of different material (polymer based instead of glass matrix) and in a different type of buffer usually comprised of ethanol, the HSE aspect needs to be evaluated for each of the new resins, safety incorporated into the SOP and operators re-trained in procedure highlighting HSE guidelines. The Material Safety Data Sheet for each product should be placed in the MSDS binder. see change action 140. Suppliers: All three suppliers, Merck Millipore, Thermo Fisher Scientific and Cytiva are approved suppliers. A re-evaluation of risk categories of these suppliers must be done, see action 138 and 144. Personnel: Operators need to receive training in the updated procedure. Action 141  Logistics: specification of incoming goods for all three alternatives needs to be prepared, see action 139 and 145. From implementation and until change action 144, 145 and 142 are completed, only Eshmuno A resin can be ordered, this is highlighted in action 155. Risks and mitigating actions: As the DEV163 study was performed with small volumes, possible issues with high pressure may appear at scale-up, exceeding pressure limits during purification. All selected medias are of the type “High- flow matrix”, which means the matrix is made to function at higher column volumes and still within pressure limits of actual scale column housing. In their respective data sheets expected back pressure build-up of 0.5-0.6 mPa over a 400mL column (height 20cm) at flowrate 45mL/min (Large column at Diatec facility). Maximum total pressure limit is 0.8 mPa. CAPA126 is initiated to monitor possible high-pressure occurrences in the next year after implementation of new protein A material, and mitigating actions will be taken on a case-by-case basis, where f and amount of crude loaded will be considered. pH and Yield. Cloneto-clone variations will always occur. As only two clones were tested, there is no guarantee that different column medias will act identically to same crude regarding pH and yield for every clone. As ligand is almost identical in all medias, they are expected to behave in a similar way, and reduce variation. In addition this is mitigated by flexible FPLC purification procedures which allows for some modifications during the purification run. This will be assessed on a case-by-case basis, and if additional modifications are needed to the process for individual clones, a separate change will be initiated to optimize the process.  Precipitation in larger-scale products can occur during purification and dialysis of different clones as only two clones were tested, and media is meant to be used for harvests from many different clones. CAPA126 is initiated to monitor the occurrence of precipitation. This is a business risk, as all not tested variations can negatively impact the delivery timeline. However, as 100 % of produced product is ongoing quality control before release, there is no significant risk to product quality.  As described above, due to strict timelines, SOP0005 will not be updated prior to implementation of new matrix material (22.jun.2023). However, as the only change to the procedures is the type of material used and the process is the same the risk of this is considered minimal. To mitigate risk to traceability and documentation, all operators trained in FPLC will receive notification on that Eschmuno A replaces ProSep vA on the 22.jun.2023 and a comment is to be made in all Doc0005.1 until SOP is updated with the new material, action 153. A separate notification will go to all trained operators in FPLC when SOP0005 is updated with the new material, to remind them that the comment is not needed anymore, action 154.  A CAPA will be raised to evaluate performance of new protein A material a year after implementation, by comparing purification with new and old column material. 2 low yield clones, 2 high yield clones, 2 clones from large scale productions and 2 clones known for being challenging to purify will be used for comparison. This will be used to identify if there are improvements needed for the process with the new protein A material, see action 143.  New protein A material can be used prior to completion of this change, after action 135-139 are completed.CMID29 must be referenced in worksheets as long as SOP0005 is not updated action 153 and 154.
Planned implementation data - comment: Change will be implemented after completion of actions 135-139, 149, 150, 153 and 155. Actions 151-152, 147, 148 and 154 will be performed after implementation, due to the expry date of current protein A purification material only Eshmuno A will be ordered and implemented immediately. This poses minimal risk, any risk identified is adressed in Risk and mitigating action section of this change.

The ASMs will follow the same maintenance regime as it is mentioned in the Standard operating procedure (SOP) for ASMs. 4. Qualification/ Validation Service has been performed on all the ASMs after they are transferred to the Diagnostics production facility. The provided service from the supplier (C3) is comprehensive and will serve as sufficient to ensure instrument function. Further qualification of instrument is not required. Service on the ASMs are performed annually and ensures the instruments performs as expected as long as the service routine is followed. 6. Training Several operators in Diagnostic production facility are already trained in using ASMs and no additional training is required. Process/product This change of ASMs are considered as like-for-like" change and does not have impact on process or quality of product. "
